> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The phrase *"minimum number of flip-flops"* in KTU questions almost always means the **binary assignment** unless the question explicitly specifies a particular coding scheme (Gray, Johnson, One-Hot, etc.).

## 1.4 Why Does This Topic Matter?

The chosen assignment directly determines:
1. The **number and complexity of Boolean equations** driving the flip-flops (excitation functions).
2. The **gate count** of the final SSI/MSI implementation or the **LUT count** in a CPLD/FPGA implementation.
3. The **propagation delay** (critical path) of the combinational block.
4. The **power consumption** of the circuit (switching activity).

For KTU B.Tech, this problem sits at the heart of **ESE Module 2 (Sequential Circuit Design)** and frequently appears in 14-mark analytical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the State Assignment Process

1. **Determine the bit-width ($n$)** required to encode $N$ states.
2. **Choose a coding rule** (Binary, Gray, One-Hot, Johnson, etc.).
3. **Construct the Transition Table** and derive the **excitation** and **output** Boolean equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Distinction**
> - **State Table** → uses *symbolic* states (a, b, c, …).
> - **Transition Table** → uses *binary-coded* states.
> - **Excitation Table** → lists flip-flop *inputs* (D, JK, T) required for each transition.
> - All three are closely related but **not identical**, and KTU frequently tests this distinction.

## 2.2 Popular State Assignment Methods

### (A) Binary (Sequential) Assignment
States are numbered $0, 1, 2, \ldots$ in natural binary order.
- **Pros:** Minimum number of flip-flops. Easy to remember.
- **Cons:** Often produces more complex Boolean equations (more prime implicants).

### (B) Gray Code Assignment
Successive states differ in only **one bit** (Hamming distance = 1).
- **Pros:** Reduces simultaneous bit transitions → less switching noise, often simpler next-state logic for "linear" FSMs.
- **Cons:** Still uses $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ flip-flops.

### (C) One-Hot Assignment
Each state has exactly **one flip-flop asserted HIGH** (only one bit = 1 at a time).
- **Pros:** Produces the **simplest** next-state and output equations (mostly AND/OR). Trivially decodable. Ideal for **FPGA/CPLD** design.
- **Cons:** Uses $N$ flip-flops (wasteful in SSI design). Illegal states must be handled (`don't-care` conditions).

### (D) Johnson (Twisted Ring) Assignment
A cyclic code with at most one bit changing per step.
- **Pros:** Useful for counters and shift-register-based state machines.
- **Cons:** Restricted to $2n$ states for $n$ flip-flops.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Minimum flip-flops | $n_{\min} = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | $N$ = number of states |
| 2 | One-hot flip-flops | $n_{OH} = N$ | Each state → 1 FF |
| 3 | Total binary codes | $2^{n}$ | With $n$ flip-flops |
| 4 | Unused codes (illegal states) | $2^{n} - N$ | Treated as `don't-cares` |
| 5 | Total raw assignments | $\dfrac{2^{n}!}{(2^{n}-N)!}$ | Permutations |
| 6 | Distinct assignments | $\dfrac{(2^{n}-1)!}{(2^{n}-N)!\cdot n!}$ | Modulo complementation & permutation |
| 7 | Output of $k$-bit Gray code at index $i$ | $G_i = B_{i+1} \oplus B_i$ | $B$ = natural binary |
| 8 | Hamming weight (One-Hot) | $= 1$ | Exactly one HIGH bit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern VLSI/FPGA design, **One-Hot encoding is the *de facto* standard** because it converts complex next-state minimization into trivial decoding logic. KTU examinations, however, expect students to master the **binary-coded transition table** as it is the textbook methodology.

## 2.4 Guidelines (Heuristic Rules) for Good State Assignment

When the "best" assignment is not obvious, the following **3 heuristic rules** (originally due to **Huffman** and **McCluskey**) are used in KTU problems:

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 1 — Adjacent States Rule**
> Two states that have the **same next state** for a given input, OR are **successive states** in the same input column, OR both appear in the **same output specification**, should be assigned **adjacent binary codes** (differ in only one bit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 2 — Output Rule**
> Two states that produce **different outputs** for the same input should be placed in **different groups** of a partition (used in Partition Theory).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 3 — Don't-Care Exploitation**
> **Unused binary codes** (illegal states) should be listed as `X` (don't-care) in the Karnaugh maps so that the Boolean equations are simplified maximally.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Traffic Light Controllers** — FSM with 4 states (Red, Yellow, Green, All-Red).
- **Vending Machines** — Coin-acceptor logic with 5–8 states.
- **Elevator Control Units** — Multi-state controllers.
- **CPU Control Units** — Hard-wired micro-programmed control.
- **Serial Communication Protocols** — UART, I²C, SPI state machines.
- **FPGA/CPLD Bitstream Controllers** — Configuration FSMs.

In every one of these, the **state assignment** choice decides the **silicon area**, **propagation delay**, and **power budget**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — From Symbolic State Table to Boolean Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark pattern):**
> A Mealy sequential machine has the following symbolic state table. The state has 4 symbols: $\{A, B, C, D\}$.
>
> | Present State | Next State, $X=0$ | Next State, $X=1$ | Output, $X=0$ | Output, $X=1$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | A | B | D | 0 | 0 |
> | B | C | D | 0 | 0 |
> | C | C | B | 0 | 1 |
> | D | A | B | 1 | 0 |
>
> *Tasks:*
> 1. Perform **Binary State Assignment** using minimum flip-flops.
> 2. Build the **Transition Table**.
> 3. Derive the **next-state** and **output** Boolean equations using K-maps.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Determine the Number of Flip-Flops

$$n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = \lceil 2 \rceil = 2$$

We need **2 flip-flops**, label them $y_1$ (MSB) and $y_2$ (LSB).

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Choose a Binary Assignment

The most common **binary (sequential) assignment** is:

$$A = 00, \quad B = 01, \quad C = 10, \quad D = 11$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Build the Transition Table

We replace every symbolic state with its binary code. The new columns are $Y_1$ (next MSB) and $Y_2$ (next LSB).

> [!NOTE]
> The "input" axis is $X$, and the "present state" axis is $y_1 y_2$. All four possible 2-bit present states are listed, including unused codes (none here, since $N = 2^n = 4$).

| Present State $y_1 y_2$ | Next State ($X=0$) $Y_1 Y_2$ | Next State ($X=1$) $Y_1 Y_2$ | Output $Z$ ($X=0$) | Output $Z$ ($X=1$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 00 (A) | 01 (B) | 11 (D) | 0 | 0 |
| 01 (B) | 10 (C) | 11 (D) | 0 | 0 |
| 10 (C) | 10 (C) | 01 (B) | 0 | 1 |
| 11 (D) | 00 (A) | 01 (B) | 1 | 0 |

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Karnaugh Map for $Y_1$

K-map for $Y_1$ (rows = $y_1 y_2$, columns = $X$):

| $y_1 y_2$ \ $X$ | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | 0 | 1 |
| 01 | 1 | 1 |
| 11 | 0 | 0 |
| 10 | 1 | 0 |

**Grouping:**
- A 2-cell group: $\{y_1 y_2 = 01, X=0\} \cup \{y_1 y_2 = 01, X=1\}$ → $\bar{y_1} y_2$.
- A 2-cell group: $\{y_1 y_2 = 10, X=0\} \cup \{y_1 y_2 = 01, X=0\}$ → $\bar{y_1}\bar{X} \cup \bar{X}y_2\bar{y_1}$ → simplifies to $\bar{X} y_2 \bar{y_1} \cup \bar{X} y_1 \bar{y_2}$ → forms an XOR-like pair.

After Boolean simplification:

$$Y_1 = \bar{y_1} y_2 + \bar{X}\, y_1 \bar{y_2}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Karnaugh Map for $Y_2$

| $y_1 y_2$ \ $X$ | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | 1 | 1 |
| 01 | 0 | 1 |
| 11 | 0 | 1 |
| 10 | 0 | 0 |

**Grouping:**
- 4-cell group covering $\{00,0\}, \{00,1\}, \{01,1\}, \{11,1\}$ → $\bar{y_1}\bar{X} + \bar{y_1}X + \bar{y_1}y_2X + y_1y_2X$ simplifies to $\bar{y_1} + y_2 X$.

After Boolean simplification:

$$Y_2 = \bar{y_1} + y_2 X$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Karnaugh Map for Output $Z$

| $y_1 y_2$ \ $X$ | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | 0 | 0 |
| 01 | 0 | 0 |
| 11 | 1 | 0 |
| 10 | 0 | 1 |

**Grouping (no adjacencies for any 2-cell grouping — single cells only):**

$$Z = y_1 y_2 \bar{X} + y_1 \bar{y_2} X$$

This can be recognized as the **XOR of $y_1$ and $(X \oplus y_2)$**:

$$Z = y_1 \oplus (X \oplus y_2)$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Verification of the Boolean Equations

Let's test row $y_1 y_2 = 11, X = 0$:
- $Y_1 = \bar{1}\cdot 1 + \bar{0}\cdot 1\cdot \bar{1} = 0 + 0 = 0$ ✓ (Next state = 00, MSB = 0)
- $Y_2 = \bar{1} + 1\cdot 0 = 0 + 0 = 0$ ✓ (Next state = 00, LSB = 0)
- $Z = 1\cdot 1 \cdot \bar{0} + 1\cdot \bar{1}\cdot 0 = 1$ ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **The same 4-state machine re-designed with One-Hot assignment** would require **4 flip-flops** ($y_A, y_B, y_C, y_D$), with codes $A=0001, B=0010, C=0100, D=1000$. The next-state equations become trivial disjunctions:

$$Y_A = X \cdot y_D, \quad Y_B = \bar{X} \cdot y_A + X \cdot y_C, \quad Y_C = \bar{X} \cdot y_B, \quad Y_D = X \cdot y_A + X \cdot y_B$$

The output $Z$ becomes:

$$Z = X \cdot y_C + \bar{X} \cdot y_D$$

| Method | FFs | NAND gates | Critical path | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Binary (sequential) | 2 | 7–8 | 3 gate delays | SSI textbook design |
| Gray code | 2 | 5–6 | 2–3 delays | Low-power FSMs |
| One-hot | 4 | 3–4 | 2 delays | FPGA / CPLD |
| Johnson | 2 | 4 | 2 delays | Counters / sequencers |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Marks are awarded for *showing every K-map cell* and the *grouping loops*. A bare equation without a K-map typically fetches only **2 out of 7 marks** in the derivation sub-part.

## 3.3 Common Pitfall — Distinguishing "Transition Table" vs. "Excitation Table"

> [!WARNING]
> **The Most Common KTU Mistake:**
> - **Transition Table** lists **next states** ($Y$) and **outputs** ($Z$).
> - **Excitation Table** lists **flip-flop inputs** (e.g., $D_1, D_2$ for D-FF, or $J_1, K_1, J_2, K_2$ for JK-FF) needed to *cause* the transition from $y \rightarrow Y$.
> For a **D flip-flop**, the excitation equals the next state: $D_i = Y_i$. For a **JK or T flip-flop**, the excitation table differs from the transition table.

| Flip-Flop Type | $Q$ (Present) | $Q^{+}$ (Next) | Excitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| D | 0 | 0 | $D = 0$ |
| D | 0 | 1 | $D = 1$ |
| D | 1 | 0 | $D = 0$ |
| D | 1 | 1 | $D = 1$ |
| JK | 0 | 0 | $J=0, K=\text{X}$ |
| JK | 0 | 1 | $J=1, K=\text{X}$ |
| JK | 1 | 0 | $J=\text{X}, K=1$ |
| JK | 1 | 1 | $J=\text{X}, K=0$ |
| T | 0 | 0 | $T=0$ |
| T | 0 | 1 | $T=1$ |
| T | 1 | 0 | $T=1$ |
| T | 1 | 1 | $T=0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FSM

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **architecture of a synchronous sequential machine**, illustrating the feedback loop that the Transition Table ultimately governs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input X"] --> B["Combinational Logic"]
    P["Present State y1 y2"] --> B
    B --> C["Excitation Functions Y1 Y2"]
    B --> D["Output Function Z"]
    C --> E["Memory Element D1 D2"]
    E --> F["Clock CLK"]
    F --> G["State Register Q1 Q2"]
    G --> H["Feedback to y1 y2"]
    H --> P
    D --> I["Output Z"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The combinational block consumes $(X, y_1, y_2)$ and produces $(Y_1, Y_2, Z)$. The flip-flops latch $Y$ on the next clock edge, and their outputs $Q_1, Q_2$ are fed back as the new present state.

## 4.2 State Assignment Mapping (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram shows how **symbolic states** are *translated* into **binary codes** before the transition table can be drawn.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0["Symbolic State Table a, b, c, d, e"] --> B0["State Assignment Mapper"]
    B0 --> C0["Binary Codes 000, 001, 010, 011, 100"]
    C0 --> D0["Transition Table"]
    D0 --> E0["K-map Simplification"]
    E0 --> F0["Boolean Equations Y1, Y2, Y3, Z"]
    F0 --> G0["Final Logic Circuit"]
```

## 4.3 State Transition Graph (Mealy Representation)

For a 3-state machine with assignment $A=00, B=01, C=10$:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> A
    A --> B: X=0, Z=0
    A --> C: X=1, Z=1
    B --> C: X=0, Z=0
    B --> B: X=1, Z=1
    C --> A: X=0, Z=1
    C --> B: X=1, Z=0
    state A { label: 00 }
    state B { label: 01 }
    state C { label: 10 }
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked to *"draw the state diagram"*, always label *every arc* with the **input/output pair** in the form `X/Z`. The arrow direction follows the present → next transition.

## 4.4 One-Hot Encoding Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["State A"] --> SA["yA = 1"]
    S1["State B"] --> SB["yB = 1"]
    S2["State C"] --> SC["yC = 1"]
    S3["State D"] --> SD["yD = 1"]
    SA --> D0["Decoder OR-gate"]
    SB --> D0
    SC --> D0
    SD --> D0
    D0 --> FF["4 D-Flip-Flops"]
    FF --> CLK["Common Clock"]
    FF --> OUT["Output Z = f(yA,yB,yC,yD)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In One-Hot encoding, only **one of the four flip-flops** is HIGH at any instant, directly indicating the current state. The next-state equations are simply OR-gates that set the appropriate FF when its predecessor state is active.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q1a]**
> **Q1.** *What is the State Assignment Problem in synchronous sequential circuit design? Why is the choice of state assignment important?*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **State Assignment Problem** is the procedure of assigning a unique binary code to each symbolic state of a reduced FSM such that the resulting **transition table** and its corresponding Boolean equations are minimal in combinational logic.
> **Importance:**
> 1. It determines the number of flip-flops and gate count.
> 2. It affects the propagation delay (critical path) of the combinational block.
> 3. It influences power dissipation due to switching activity.
> A "good" assignment can reduce a 7-gate circuit to a 3-gate circuit using the same flip-flop technology.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1c]**
> **Q2.** *Differentiate between a State Table and a Transition Table with an example.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | State Table | Transition Table |
> |---|---|---|
> | States | Symbolic (a, b, c…) | Binary-coded (00, 01, 10…) |
> | Pre-design step | Used *before* assignment | Used *after* assignment |
> | Readability | Easy for the designer | Suitable for hardware realization |
> | Example | A, B, C | 00, 01, 10 |
> Example: A state table with $A \rightarrow B$ on input $X=0$ becomes $00 \rightarrow 01$ in the transition table after binary assignment.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q2(a–b) Pattern]**
>
> **Question A.** *(a)* The symbolic state table of a Mealy machine is given below. *(7 marks)*
>
> | Present State | Next State ($X=0$) | Next State ($X=1$) | Output ($X=0$) | Output ($X=1$) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | $S_0$ | $S_0$ | $S_2$ | 0 | 0 |
> | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_0$ | 0 | 1 |
> | $S_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_3$ | 0 | 0 |
> | $S_3$ | $S_3$ | $S_1$ | 1 | 0 |
>
> Perform **binary state assignment** with $S_0=00, S_1=01, S_2=10, S_3=11$ and obtain the **Transition Table**.
>
> *(b)* Derive the **next-state** and **output** Boolean equations using K-maps. Use **D flip-flops**. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Transition Table Construction (7 marks):**

Number of flip-flops:
$$n = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$$
Label the flip-flops as $y_1$ (MSB) and $y_2$ (LSB).

The transition table (with binary assignment) is:

| Row | Present State $y_1 y_2$ | Next State ($X=0$) | Next State ($X=1$) | $Z$ ($X=0$) | $Z$ ($X=1$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 00 ($S_0$) | 00 ($S_0$) | 10 ($S_2$) | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 01 ($S_1$) | 10 ($S_2$) | 00 ($S_0$) | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | 10 ($S_2$) | 01 ($S_1$) | 11 ($S_3$) | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 11 ($S_3$) | 11 ($S_3$) | 01 ($S_1$) | 1 | 0 |

(5 marks for the table itself; 2 marks for the introductory count of flip-flops.)

**Part (b) — K-map Derivation (7 marks):**

For **D flip-flops**, $D_1 = Y_1$ and $D_2 = Y_2$.

**K-map for $Y_1$ (and hence $D_1$):**

| $y_1 y_2$ \ $X$ | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | 0 | 1 |
| 01 | 1 | 0 |
| 11 | 1 | 0 |
| 10 | 0 | 1 |

Groups: Two 2-cell groups diagonally → pairs with Hamming distance 2.
$$D_1 = Y_1 = \bar{y_1}\bar{y_2}X + y_1 y_2 \bar{X} + y_1\bar{y_2}\bar{X} + \bar{y_1}y_2 X$$

After Boolean simplification using consensus theorem:
$$D_1 = Y_1 = (y_1 \oplus y_2) \oplus X$$

**K-map for $Y_2$ (and hence $D_2$):**

| $y_1 y_2$ \ $X$ | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | 0 | 0 |
| 01 | 0 | 0 |
| 11 | 1 | 1 |
| 10 | 1 | 1 |

Two 4-cell groups:
$$D_2 = Y_2 = y_1 y_2 + y_1 \bar{y_2} = y_1$$

**K-map for Output $Z$:**

| $y_1 y_2$ \ $X$ | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | 0 | 0 |
| 01 | 0 | 1 |
| 11 | 1 | 0 |
| 10 | 0 | 0 |

Single cells, no groups:
$$Z = y_1 y_2 \bar{X} + \bar{y_1} y_2 X = y_2(y_1 \oplus X)' \cdot \text{... wait, reformulated as } Z = y_2 (y_1 \oplus X)$$

$$\boxed{Z = y_2 (y_1 \oplus X)}$$

(2 marks for $D_1$, 2 marks for $D_2$, 3 marks for $Z$ with K-maps shown.)

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q3 Pattern]**
>
> **Question B.** *(a)* Explain the **State Assignment Problem** and discuss the **binary, Gray code, and one-hot** assignment methods with their relative merits. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A Mealy machine has 3 states $\{A, B, C\}$. Using **One-Hot assignment**, draw the **state diagram** and write the **transition table**. Then derive the next-state equations for D flip-flops. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Concept (7 marks):**

(2 marks) The State Assignment Problem is the procedure of assigning unique binary codes of $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ bits to each of the $N$ states of a reduced FSM.

(5 marks) Three methods:

| Method | # FFs | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binary | $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Minimum FFs, simple | More complex Boolean logic |
| Gray code | $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | One bit changes per state → low noise | Still requires minimization |
| One-hot | $N$ | Trivial next-state equations; ideal for FPGA | Many FFs, must handle illegal states |

**Part (b) — One-Hot Design (7 marks):**

For 3 states, One-Hot requires **3 flip-flops**: $y_A, y_B, y_C$, with codes:
- $A = 100$ ($y_A=1$, others 0)
- $B = 010$
- $C = 001$

State diagram (text representation):

$$A \xrightarrow{X=0/Z=0} A, \quad A \xrightarrow{X=1/Z=1} B$$
$$B \xrightarrow{X=0/Z=1} C, \quad B \xrightarrow{X=1/Z=0} A$$
$$C \xrightarrow{X=0/Z=0} B, \quad C \xrightarrow{X=1/Z=1} C$$

Transition table:

| $y_A y_B y_C$ | $X$ | $Y_A Y_B Y_C$ | $Z$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 0 | 100 | 0 |
| 100 | 1 | 010 | 1 |
| 010 | 0 | 001 | 1 |
| 010 | 1 | 100 | 0 |
| 001 | 0 | 010 | 0 |
| 001 | 1 | 001 | 1 |

Next-state equations (for D-FFs, $D_i = Y_i$):

$$D_A = Y_A = y_A \bar{X} + y_B X$$

$$D_B = Y_B = y_A X + y_C \bar{X}$$

$$D_C = Y_C = y_B \bar{X} + y_C X$$

Output equation:
$$Z = y_A X + y_B \bar{X} + y_C X = y_A X + y_C X + y_B \bar{X} = X(y_A + y_C) + \bar{X} y_B$$

(2 marks for the state diagram, 2 marks for the transition table, 3 marks for the equations.)

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing State Table and Transition Table (–1 to –2 marks):** A common error is to present the symbolic state table as the "transition table". The examiner looks for *binary-coded* present/next states.
>
> 2. **Wrong Number of Flip-Flops (–2 marks):** Many students skip the $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ calculation and directly write down a table. If the FF count is missing or wrong, partial credit is lost.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Mark Don't-Cares (–1 to –3 marks):** When $2^n > N$, the extra rows in the K-map *must* be marked `X`. Omitting this often leads to over-complicated equations.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up Mealy and Moore Outputs (–2 marks):** In a **Mealy** machine, output is a function of $(X, y)$. In a **Moore** machine, output depends only on $y$. The K-map must be drawn accordingly — the Mealy K-map has *both* $X$ and $y$ as inputs, while the Moore K-map has *only* $y$.
>
> 5. **Not Specifying the Flip-Flop Type (–1 mark):** Always state explicitly: *"Using D flip-flops"* or *"Using JK flip-flops"*. The excitation equations differ.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Verification Step (–1 mark):** Substitute one row of the transition table back into the Boolean equations to verify correctness. This single line often fetches the *grace mark* between 13 and 14.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **State Assignment Problem** = assigning unique binary codes to symbolic states to minimize logic.
> - **Transition Table** = binary-coded version of the state table.
> - **Minimum flip-flops:** $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.
> - **One-hot flip-flops:** $n = N$.
> - **Distinct assignments:** $\dfrac{(2^n - 1)!}{(2^n - N)! \cdot n!}$ (this is a *very* high-yield formula for KTU 2-marker questions).
> - **Three heuristic rules:** Adjacent States, Output Grouping, Don't-Care Exploitation.
> - **For D-FF:** $D_i = Y_i$ (excitation = next state).
> - **For JK-FF:** Use the JK excitation table: $00 \rightarrow 0$, $01 \rightarrow X$, $10 \rightarrow X$, $11 \rightarrow 1$.
> - **For T-FF:** $T = Q \oplus Q^+$ (toggle when state changes).
> - **Moore vs. Mealy:** Moore output depends only on *present* state ($Z = f(y)$); Mealy depends on *present state + input* ($Z = f(X, y)$).
> - **Illegal states** are always `don't-cares` in the K-map.
> - **One-Hot** is preferred for FPGA; **Binary** is preferred for SSI textbook design.
> - **Gray code** minimizes simultaneous bit transitions.
> - Always **verify** the Boolean equations by substituting one row of the transition table.
> - The **state diagram** must be drawn with **input/output** labels on every arc.
> - **Order of solution:** (1) Count FFs → (2) Assign codes → (3) Build transition table → (4) K-map → (5) Equations → (6) Verify.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Asynchronous Sequential Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT402 - DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Asynchronous Sequential Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Design of Asynchronous Sequential Circuits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Asynchronous Sequential Circuit** is a sequential digital circuit whose state transitions are governed directly by the input signals without the aid of a global synchronization clock. Transitions occur the moment any input changes, and the next state is a function of the present state and the *current* (un-clocked) inputs. Because there is no clock edge to define discrete instants, the design is fundamentally built around the concept of **stable states** — states in which the present state equals the next state for the given input combination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> An asynchronous sequential circuit is a sequential circuit that does not employ a clock signal to synchronize state changes. The circuit transitions freely in response to input changes, and the design must guarantee that only one internal variable changes at a time (or change in a controlled, cycle-free manner) to avoid critical races and hazards.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

- **Internal State (y):** The binary value currently stored in the feedback path (the flip-flop equivalent memory element).
- **Input State (x):** The current value of the external primary inputs.
- **Total State (y, x):** The combination of the internal state and the input state — a column entry in the flow table.
- **Stable State:** A total state in which **Y (next state) = y (present state)** for a given input. The circuit is "at rest" in that state.
- **Unstable State:** A total state in which **Y ≠ y** for a given input. The circuit will move to another row on the next internal delay.
- **Primitive Flow Table:** The most fundamental (and largest) flow table that contains exactly one stable state for each row, capturing every conceivable input change as a separate stable state.
- **Merged Flow Table:** A reduced flow table in which several rows of the primitive table are combined to minimize circuit complexity.
- **Flow Table:** A tabular representation of the asynchronous sequential machine showing next-state and output behavior for every (state, input) combination.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Automatic Sliding Door"**
> Imagine a supermarket sliding door with two motion sensors: `A` (entrance side) and `B` (exit side). The door's *internal state* is `Open` or `Closed`. The *input state* is the (A, B) combination. The door opens the moment someone triggers A and closes after they pass B — there is no clock telling it "check every second." It reacts *asynchronously* the instant an input changes.
>
> If the door is in state `Open` and the inputs are (A=0, B=0), it must stay open — that is a **stable total state**. But if inputs change to (A=1, B=0) and the door is still mechanically in the `Closed` state, the *next state* says "Open," so the door must transition — that is an **unstable total state**. The asynchronous design problem is exactly this: define the *flow table* of stable states, then build combinational logic that drives the door from unstable to stable as fast as possible without bouncing (no **hazards**).

### 1.4 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Timing element | Clocked flip-flops | Unclocked latches / pure gates with feedback |
| State change | Only on clock edge | Any time inputs change |
| State count in table | One row per state | One row per stable total state |
| Primary design hazard | Setup/hold time | Hazards, races, cycles |
| Output | Moore or Mealy (clocked) | Usually fundamental-mode Mealy |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *asynchronous* with *combinational* circuits. They are **not** the same. Asynchronous sequential circuits have **memory** (feedback) but no **clock**. A purely combinational circuit has no memory at all.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Asynchronous Design Procedure (Standard KTU 7-Step Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies a **seven-step canonical design procedure**. Every exam answer must follow this exact order to earn full marks.

1. **Obtain a verbal / word description** of the desired circuit behavior.
2. **Construct a Primitive Flow Table** by interpreting the word problem: introduce one stable state for each permissible input sequence.
3. **Reduce the Primitive Flow Table** by merging equivalent rows (merging procedure based on output compatibility and state equivalence).
4. **Assign binary state variables** using a race-free (or critical-race-free) state assignment.
5. **Construct the Transition Table** and the **Output Table** (K-map form).
6. **Derive the Boolean excitation / next-state functions** $Y_i = f_i(y_1, y_2, \dots, x_1, x_2, \dots)$ and the output function $Z = g(y_1, y_2, \dots, x_1, x_2, \dots)$.
7. **Draw the logic diagram** with feedback, ensuring **hazard-free** implementation.

### 2.2 Detailed Explanation of Each Step

#### Step 1 — Word Description
A clear, unambiguous description of the circuit's input–output behavior is the foundation. The KTU paper often gives this directly (e.g., "Design a circuit with two inputs $A$ and $B$ such that the output becomes 1 only when $A$ becomes 1 followed by $B$ becoming 1, and resets when $A$ returns to 0").

#### Step 2 — Primitive Flow Table Construction
For an $n$-input machine, there are $2^n$ possible input columns. For each *meaningful* input sequence, a separate stable state is created. Each row in the primitive table represents **one stable state**. The entries in the cells are either:
- The **name of the row** (indicating a stable state), or
- The **name of the next row** the circuit must move to (indicating an unstable transition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Don't-care entries:** In the primitive flow table, cells whose behavior is not specified by the problem statement are marked as **dashes (–)**. These become powerful don't-cares during merging and minimization.

#### Step 3 — Merging of Flow Table (Reduction)
Two rows can be merged if and only if:
1. They have **identical outputs** in every column (or compatible outputs under merging rules), AND
2. Their **next-state entries** are the same in every column, OR point to each other (or to a third merged row), OR are don't-cares.

The process uses the **merging rule theorem**: Two stable states in different rows can be merged into a single row if, for every input column, their next-state entries do not conflict (i.e., both are stable in their own rows, or both transition to the same next state, or one or both are don't-cares).

The result is the **merged flow table**, which is the minimal form of the primitive table.

#### Step 4 — State Assignment
A merged state must be assigned a unique binary code using the minimum number of variables. The number of flip-flop equivalents (latches) needed is:

$$n = \lceil \log_2(m) \rceil$$

where $m$ is the number of merged states. The assignment must be **race-free**. A *critical race* occurs when, during a transition between two states whose binary codes differ in more than one bit, an intermediate unstable state (caused by unequal propagation delays) is misinterpreted as the final state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Safe State Assignment Rule:** Assign binary codes such that any two states that share a *common successor* or that the circuit must traverse through during a transition have codes that differ in only **one bit** (Gray-code like). This guarantees *non-critical races* only (or complete race-freedom if cycles are eliminated).

#### Step 5 — Transition Table & Output Table
Substitute the binary codes into the merged flow table:
- **Transition Table:** $Y_i$ as a function of $(y_1, y_2, \dots, x_1, x_2, \dots)$.
- **Output Table:** $Z$ as a function of $(y_1, y_2, \dots, x_1, \dots)$.

Both are essentially **K-maps** with don't-cares filled in for unused total states.

#### Step 6 — Boolean Excitation Equations
Use K-map simplification to extract the minimal sum-of-products (SOP) expressions. For asynchronous circuits, K-map simplification must additionally consider the **adjacency of present-state cells** to ensure hazard-free covering (see §2.4 below).

#### Step 7 — Logic Diagram
Draw the AND-OR (or NAND-NAND) realization with feedback lines from outputs $Y_i$ back to the inputs as $y_i$. Add a buffer on the feedback path if a race-through (dynamic hazard) is detected.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of latches | $n = \lceil \log_2 m \rceil$ | $m$ = merged states, $n$ = state variables | State-assignment sizing |
| 2 | Stable state condition | $Y_i = y_i$ for all $i$ | $Y_i$ = next state, $y_i$ = present state | Stability check |
| 3 | Unstable state condition | $Y_i \neq y_i$ for at least one $i$ | — | Transition trigger |
| 4 | Total state encoding | $TS = (y_1 y_2 \dots y_n, x_1 x_2 \dots x_k)$ | $n$ state bits, $k$ input bits | Flow-table row identifier |
| 5 | Fundamental mode | Only **one** input changes at a time | — | Asynchronous design assumption |
| 6 | Static-1 hazard | $Z \to 0 \to 1 \to 0$ glitch on constant 1 output | — | Hazard identification |
| 7 | Static-0 hazard | $Z \to 1 \to 0 \to 1$ glitch on constant 0 output | — | Hazard identification |
| 8 | Dynamic hazard | $Z \to 1 \to 0 \to 1$ (or vice-versa) on a single transition | — | Output-only glitches |
| 9 | Essential hazard | Caused by unequal path delays through feedback loop | — | Must fix with delay matching |
| 10 | Race condition | $Y_i$ changes while $Y_j$ still in transit | $i \neq j$ | Critical race if codes differ ≥ 2 bits |

### 2.4 Hazards in Asynchronous Circuits (KTU High-Yield Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hazards are the **single most-tested** concept on asynchronous design in KTU papers. A hazard is a *transient* (temporary) incorrect output produced during a single input transition.

There are three types:

1. **Static Hazard:** Output is *supposed* to remain constant (either at 0 or 1) but glitches.
   - *Static-1 hazard:* Output briefly drops to 0.
   - *Static-0 hazard:* Output briefly rises to 1.
2. **Dynamic Hazard:** Output changes more than once during a single transition (e.g., supposed to go 0→1 but instead goes 0→1→0→1).
3. **Essential Hazard:** Caused by unequal propagation delays around a feedback loop. Cannot be fixed by logic redesign; must be fixed by *delay matching* in the feedback path.

**Hazard-free SOP covering rule:** When simplifying a K-map, the minimal expression must include **all prime implicants that cover the input transition in question**. Missing a prime implicant that crosses the transition boundary creates a static hazard. Adding a redundant (consensus) term eliminates it.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Asynchronous design is the foundation of:
- **Asynchronous (clockless) CPUs** (e.g., the AMULET processors, Intel's experimental Rops).
- **Handshake protocols** between independent clocked blocks (request/acknowledge).
- **Glitch-free control logic** in low-power IoT sensors where running a clock is impossible.
- **Debounce circuits** for mechanical switches.
- **Arbiters and arbiters in NoC** (network-on-chip) routers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Design Example (Classic KTU 14-Mark Format)

> **Problem:** Design an asynchronous sequential circuit with two inputs $A$ and $B$ and one output $Z$. The output $Z$ should become 1 when $A = 1$ followed by $B = 1$ (in that order, without $A$ returning to 0 in between). Once $Z = 1$, the output remains 1 even if $B$ toggles. The output returns to 0 when $A$ returns to 0.

### Step 1 — Word Description Analysis

The required input sequence is $A = 1$, *then* $B = 1$. We must track *two* pieces of information: (i) whether $A$ has occurred, and (ii) whether $B$ has occurred after $A$. Hence, the minimal machine has **3 distinct states**:
- **State 1:** Initial state — $A$ has not yet become 1.
- **State 2:** $A$ has become 1, but $B$ has not yet become 1.
- **State 3:** $A = 1$ *and* $B = 1$ has occurred (output is 1).

However, because the inputs are asynchronous, we need separate stable states for each *input combination the circuit is permitted to rest in*. Let us derive the primitive flow table systematically.

### Step 2 — Primitive Flow Table

There are $2^2 = 4$ input columns: $AB = 00, 01, 10, 11$.

Let us identify all valid input sequences the circuit can be in:

- **Row 1 (State 1, Initial):** Stable when $A = 0$ regardless of $B$.
- **Row 2 (State 2, $A=1$ first):** Stable when $A = 1$ and $B = 0$.
- **Row 3 (State 3, Output = 1):** Stable when $A = 1$ and $B = 1$.
- **Row 4 (Reset transient):** We need a row for $A = 0$ that allows the output to be reset.

Therefore, the primitive flow table (showing row state name in each stable cell, dash in unspecified cells) is:

| Present State (Row) | $AB=00$ | $AB=01$ | $AB=10$ | $AB=11$ | Output $Z$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** (Initial) | (1) | – | (1) | – | 0 |
| **2** ($A=1$ seen) | 3 | – | (2) | 1 | 0 |
| **3** ($AB=11$ achieved) | 4 | – | 2 | (3) | 1 |
| **4** (Reset) | (4) | – | 3 | – | 0 |

**Reading the table:** A circled number indicates a stable state. An un-circled number indicates a transition to that row on the next internal delay.

- Cell (Row 1, $AB=00$): Circuit is in initial state, input is 00 → **stable** → mark as **(1)**.
- Cell (Row 1, $AB=10$): Initial state, but $A$ just became 1 → must go to State 2 → mark as **2**.
- Cell (Row 2, $AB=10$): We are in "A seen" state, input is $AB=10$ → **stable** → **(2)**.
- Cell (Row 2, $AB=11$): $A=1$ and $B=1$ → must go to State 3 → mark as **3**.
- Cell (Row 3, $AB=11$): Output should be 1 → **stable** → **(3)** with $Z=1$.
- Cell (Row 3, $AB=10$): If $B$ returns to 0, we should stay in "A seen" state → go to State 2 → mark as **2**.
- Cell (Row 3, $AB=00$): $A$ returned to 0 → reset to State 4 (output goes to 0) → mark as **4**.
- Cell (Row 4, $AB=00$): **stable** in reset state → **(4)** with $Z=0$.
- Cell (Row 4, $AB=10$): If $A$ becomes 1 again from reset → go to State 2 → mark as **2**.

### Step 3 — Merging the Primitive Flow Table

We check each pair of rows for merge compatibility using the rules from §2.2:

- **Rows 1 and 4:** Outputs are both 0. Check columns:
  - $AB=00$: Both stable (1) and (4) — entries are 1 and 4 (compatible, no conflict — they point to *themselves* in the merged row).
  - $AB=10$: Row 1 points to 2, Row 4 points to 2 — **identical** → compatible.
  - Other columns: Don't-cares — compatible.
  - ✅ **Rows 1 and 4 can be merged.**

- **Rows 1 and 2:** $AB=00$ has entries 1 and 3 (conflict — they point to *different* rows). ❌ Cannot merge directly.

- **Rows 2 and 4:** $AB=00$ has 3 and (4). The "3" is unstable and points to row 4, while (4) is stable. Under the merge rule, an unstable entry pointing to a stable entry of the *other* row is compatible with a stable entry. Let us re-check: the merged cell becomes the state of the *destination*. So 3 in row 2 at $AB=00$ and (4) in row 4 at $AB=00$ are compatible (both effectively become "4" in the merged row).
  - $AB=10$: Row 2 is (2), Row 4 points to 2 → (2) and 2 are compatible (becomes (2)).
  - ✅ **Rows 2 and 4 can be merged.**

- **Rows 2 and 3:** $AB=11$ has (2) and (3) — both stable in different rows. Conflict. ❌ Cannot merge (different outputs are also a problem — row 2 has $Z=0$, row 3 has $Z=1$).

- **Rows 1 and 3:** Different outputs. ❌ Cannot merge.

- **Rows 3 and 4:** Different outputs. ❌ Cannot merge.

So the compatible merge sets are: {1, 4} and {2, 4}. But we need a **maximal compatibility class** (covering all rows with no conflict). We can pick:
- **Merged Row A** = {1, 4}
- **Merged Row B** = {2}
- **Merged Row C** = {3}

This is a valid 3-row merged flow table. (Alternative: merge {2,4} and keep {1} and {3} — also valid, but {1,4} merging is cleaner.)

### Step 4 — Merged Flow Table

Let us rename the merged rows: $\alpha = \{1,4\}$, $\beta = 2$, $\gamma = 3$.

| Merged State | $AB=00$ | $AB=01$ | $AB=10$ | $AB=11$ | Output $Z$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$\alpha$** | ($\alpha$) | – | $\beta$ | – | 0 |
| **$\beta$** | $\alpha$ | – | ($\beta$) | $\gamma$ | 0 |
| **$\gamma$** | $\alpha$ | – | $\beta$ | ($\gamma$) | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of stability:**
> - Cell ($\alpha$, $AB=00$): present state $\alpha$, next state $\alpha$ → stable. ✅
> - Cell ($\beta$, $AB=10$): present state $\beta$, next state $\beta$ → stable. ✅
> - Cell ($\gamma$, $AB=11$): present state $\gamma$, next state $\gamma$ → stable. ✅
> - All other cells with non-matching present/next states are correctly **unstable**.

### Step 5 — State Assignment (Race-Free)

Number of merged states: $m = 3$. Number of state variables needed:

$$n = \lceil \log_2 3 \rceil = 2$$

Two state variables $y_1$ and $y_2$. We assign:
- $\alpha = 00$
- $\beta = 01$
- $\gamma = 10$

(Code 11 is unused — a powerful don't-care for the K-map.)

**Race-freeness check:** Transitions are $\alpha \leftrightarrow \beta$ (differs in 1 bit ✅), $\beta \leftrightarrow \gamma$ (differs in 1 bit ✅), $\alpha \leftrightarrow \gamma$ (differs in 2 bits — but this transition does not occur in any single input change). The machine is **race-free** under this assignment.

### Step 6 — Transition Table & Output Table

Substituting the binary codes:

| $y_1 y_2$ | $AB=00$ | $AB=01$ | $AB=10$ | $AB=11$ | $Z$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **00** ($\alpha$) | **00** | – | 01 | – | 0 |
| **01** ($\beta$) | 00 | – | **01** | 10 | 0 |
| **10** ($\gamma$) | 00 | – | 01 | **10** | 1 |
| **11** (unused) | – | – | – | – | – |

**Output Table (Z):**

| $y_1 y_2$ | $A$ | $B$ | $Z$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 00 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 00 | 0 | 1 | – |
| 00 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 00 | 1 | 1 | – |
| 01 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 01 | 0 | 1 | – |
| 01 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 01 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 10 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 10 | 0 | 1 | – |
| 10 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 10 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 11 | x | x | – |

### Step 7 — Boolean Excitation Equations

The transition table gives $Y_1$ and $Y_2$ as functions of $(y_1, y_2, A, B)$.

**K-map for $Y_1$** (entries in $y_1 y_2$ rows, $AB$ columns):

| | $00$ | $01$ | $11$ | $10$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y_1 y_2 = 00$ | 0 | – | – | 0 |
| $01$ | 0 | – | 1 | 0 |
| $11$ | – | – | – | – |
| $10$ | 0 | – | 1 | 0 |

Simplifying $Y_1$:

$$Y_1 = A \cdot y_2 + A \cdot y_1$$

> **Logic check:** $Y_1$ becomes 1 only when $A=1$ AND the present state is either $\beta$ (01) or $\gamma$ (10) — i.e., when $y_1 \neq y_2$. This is the moment we are *past* the initial state and $A$ is high. ✅

**K-map for $Y_2$** (entries in $y_1 y_2$ rows, $AB$ columns):

| | $00$ | $01$ | $11$ | $10$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y_1 y_2 = 00$ | 0 | – | – | 1 |
| $01$ | 0 | – | 0 | 1 |
| $11$ | – | – | – | – |
| $10$ | 0 | – | 0 | 1 |

Simplifying $Y_2$:

$$Y_2 = A \cdot \overline{y_1}$$

> **Logic check:** $Y_2$ becomes 1 when $A=1$ and the present state is **not** $\gamma$ — i.e., when $A$ just arrived but the output is not yet 1. This corresponds to going from $\alpha$ to $\beta$. ✅

**K-map for output $Z$:**

From the output table, $Z = 1$ only at $y_1 y_2 = 10, AB = 11$:

$$Z = A \cdot B \cdot y_1$$

### Step 8 — Final Boolean Equations (Summary)

$$\boxed{Y_1 = A y_1 + A y_2 = A(y_1 + y_2)}$$

$$\boxed{Y_2 = A \cdot \overline{y_1}}$$

$$\boxed{Z = A \cdot B \cdot y_1}$$

### Step 9 — Hazard Analysis

**Static-1 hazard check on $Y_2 = A \cdot \overline{y_1}$:** Consider the input transition $A: 0 \to 1$ while $y_1 = 0$.
- At $A=0, y_1=0$: $Y_2 = 0 \cdot 1 = 0$.
- At $A=1, y_1=0$: $Y_2 = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$.
- During transition, $A$ may briefly be 0.5 (interpreted as 0) — no glitch.

Now consider $y_1: 0 \to 1$ while $A = 1$:
- At $y_1=0, A=1$: $Y_2 = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$.
- At $y_1=1, A=1$: $Y_2 = 1 \cdot 0 = 0$.

This is a legitimate state change, not a hazard.

**Static-1 hazard check on $Y_1 = A(y_1 + y_2)$:** Consider the input transition $y_1: 0 \to 1$ while $A=1, y_2=0$:
- At $y_1=0, y_2=0, A=1$: $Y_1 = 1 \cdot (0+0) = 0$.
- At $y_1=1, y_2=0, A=1$: $Y_1 = 1 \cdot (1+0) = 1$.
- During transition, if $y_1$ goes to 0 first and $y_2$ to 0 still — $Y_1$ stays 0. No glitch here. ✅

However, consider the transition $\beta \to \gamma$: $(y_1 y_2)$ goes from $01$ to $10$. Both bits change. The *intermediate* state could be $00$ (if $y_1$ changes first) or $11$ (if $y_2$ changes first). State $00$ is $\alpha$, but the machine was *supposed* to go to $\gamma$ (10). This is a **non-critical race** (in this case, $00$ reverts back to $\beta$ via the unstable entry, then to $\gamma$). To eliminate it, we can add the redundant term $\overline{y_1} y_2$ — but as we noted, the assignment already tolerates the race because no third stable state is hit.

For absolute safety, the KTU-accepted solution often adds the consensus term:

$$Y_1 = A y_1 + A y_2 + y_1 y_2 \text{ (with don't-cares optimized)}$$

### Step 10 — Logic Diagram Specification

The final circuit has:
- 2 feedback paths ($y_1, y_2$).
- 1 output $Z$.
- 2 input lines ($A, B$).
- AND-OR (or NAND-NAND) gates implementing $Y_1, Y_2, Z$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final answer for the 14-mark KTU question** would include all of: (a) Primitive flow table, (b) Merged flow table, (c) State assignment justification, (d) Transition and output tables, (e) Boolean equations, (f) Logic diagram, and (g) Hazard analysis. Every step must be present and labeled.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Race vs. Hazard vs. Cycle

The three "dangers" of asynchronous design are often confused. This is the KTU exam discriminator:

| Phenomenon | Cause | Effect | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Hazard** | Single input change → output *should* stay constant but glitches | Spurious short pulse on output | Add redundant prime implicant to cover the transition |
| **Dynamic Hazard** | Multi-level logic + multiple input changes | Output changes multiple times when it should change once | Use two-level logic, OR add delay buffers |
| **Essential Hazard** | Unequal path delays around a feedback loop | Output settles to wrong state after a single input change | Match delays in feedback path |
| **Critical Race** | State codes differ in ≥ 2 bits, unequal internal delays | Machine reaches a wrong stable state | Use race-free state assignment (adjacency-based) |
| **Non-Critical Race** | State codes differ in ≥ 2 bits, but intermediate state is unstable | Machine takes a longer path, but arrives at the correct state | Usually tolerable, but slow |
| **Cycle** | Two unstable states chasing each other | Output oscillates forever | Detect in flow table; break the cycle by state reassignment |
| **Deadlock** | All next states are don't-care or stuck | Machine never transitions out of a state | Add explicit reset logic |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Quick Win:** Always include a *race/hazard analysis* in your design answer, even if the question does not explicitly ask. Examiners reward this with 1–2 extra marks.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Asynchronous Design Flow (Block Diagram)

The complete 7-step process is captured in this Mermaid block-level flow diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Word Description of Circuit] --> primA[Step 2: Build Primitive Flow Table]
    primA --> redA[Step 3: Reduce Merge Rows]
    redA --> assignA[Step 4: Race-Free State Assignment]
    assignA --> transA[Step 5: Transition and Output Tables]
    transA --> boolA[Step 6: Boolean Excitation Equations]
    boolA --> logicA[Step 7: Logic Diagram with Feedback]
    logicA --> hazardA{Hazard Analysis}
    hazardA -- hazards found --> fixA[Add Redundant Prime Implicants]
    fixA --> logicA
    hazardA -- no hazards --> finalA[Final Hazard-Free Circuit]
```

## 4.2 State-Transition Map (Mermaid Visualization)

The following diagram visualizes the *stable total states* and their permitted input-driven transitions for the worked example:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> alpha
    alpha --> beta : A becomes 1, B=0
    beta --> alpha : A becomes 0
    beta --> gamma : A=1, B becomes 1
    gamma --> beta : A=1, B becomes 0
    gamma --> alpha : A becomes 0
    note right of alpha : Z = 0
    note right of beta : Z = 0
    note right of gamma : Z = 1
```

## 4.3 Final Logic Block Diagram (Architecture View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input A] --> and1[AND gate Y1 term 1]
    B[Input B] --> and3[AND gate Z term]
    y1[Feedback y1] --> and1
    y1 --> and2[AND gate Y1 term 2]
    y2[Feedback y2] --> and2
    and1 --> or1[OR gate]
    and2 --> or1
    or1 --> Y1[Output Y1 to next-state latch]
    A --> and4[AND gate Y2]
    y1 --> inv1[NOT gate]
    inv1 --> and4
    and4 --> Y2[Output Y2 to next-state latch]
    y1 --> and3
    and3 --> Z[Output Z]
    Y1 --> y1
    Y2 --> y2
```

## 4.4 K-Map Layout for $Y_1$ and $Y_2$ (Visual Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    km1[K-Map for Y1]
    km2[K-Map for Y2]
    km3[K-Map for Z]
    km1 --> simp1[Simplify: Y1 = A y1 + A y2]
    km2 --> simp2[Simplify: Y2 = A y1-bar]
    km3 --> simp3[Simplify: Z = A B y1]
    simp1 --> eqbox1[Final Equation Box: Y1]
    simp2 --> eqbox2[Final Equation Box: Y2]
    simp3 --> eqbox3[Final Equation Box: Z]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The above Mermaid block diagrams are not physical wiring diagrams (which would require logic-gate-level schematics best done in tools like Logisim). They represent the *architectural flow* of the design process and the *information flow* between the major blocks of the asynchronous machine — exactly what KTU 2024 scheme answers are graded on.
