- **Single-gate and 2-level SOP/POS networks** are **immune to dynamic hazards** but **NOT automatically immune to static hazards** — explicit K-map check required.
- **XOR function** $A \oplus B$ cannot be made hazard-free in 2-level NAND form because its 1-cells are **diagonal, not adjacent** on the K-map.
- **Hazard-free design** is mandatory in **aerospace, medical, and railway signalling** applications; in consumer electronics, dynamic hazards are often tolerable if downstream registers have sufficient setup time margin.
- **Industry tools** (Synopsys, Cadence) automatically perform hazard analysis using **timing simulation** and **formal verification** — but understanding the underlying K-map principle is essential for the KTU Board exam.
- **Mark allocation:** In Part A, expect 3 marks for definition + classification. In Part B, expect 7 marks for (a) realisation + detection and 7 marks for (b) explanation + elimination with K-map sketch.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Essential Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT402 - DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Essential Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **Essential Hazard** is a type of timing fault that occurs **exclusively in asynchronous sequential circuits**, where a single change in an input variable causes the output (or the secondary/feedback variables) to transition through an **incorrect sequence of states** before eventually settling into the correct stable state. This wrong sequence is fundamentally produced because the input transition propagates back to the feedback variables through **two or more paths having unequal (and uncompensated) propagation delays** inside the feedback loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Essential hazards are *NOT* present in pure combinational logic. They cannot be removed by classical hazard-covering techniques (such as adding consensus/redundant prime implicants that fix static-1 and static-0 hazards). They are a structural consequence of asynchronous feedback architecture.

## 1.2 Family of Hazards – Quick Comparison

| Hazard Type | Domain | Caused By | Typical Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static-0 Hazard | Combinational | Single input change, output should remain at 0 | Add redundant prime implicant |
| Static-1 Hazard | Combinational | Single input change, output should remain at 1 | Add redundant prime implicant |
| Dynamic Hazard | Multi-level combinational | Single input change causing multiple output changes | Redesign as 2-level SOP/POS |
| **Essential Hazard** | **Asynchronous Sequential** | **Unequal feedback path delays** | **Insert/Adjust delay elements** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Two-Way Mirror Traffic Signal"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine two traffic policemen, **Policeman-A** and **Policeman-B**, who are supposed to coordinate using hand signals sent through **two different tunnels** of different lengths. The driver (the feedback variable) needs both signals simultaneously to make a correct turn. When the input (an approaching vehicle) sends a single "Go" signal, Policeman-A's signal arrives through the short tunnel in **3 seconds**, while Policeman-B's signal arrives through the long tunnel in **8 seconds**. The driver briefly takes the **wrong lane** before finally settling into the correct one. That brief, erroneous maneuver is exactly analogous to an **essential hazard**.

The *root cause* is **asymmetry of propagation delay** in the feedback loop — not the logic expression itself.

## 1.4 Why It Is Called "Essential"

It is called *essential* because, unlike static hazards, the hazard is **intrinsic to the feedback structure** of the asynchronous machine. Changing the Boolean simplification, removing redundant gates, or covering with consensus terms will **not eliminate** it. The hazard is **essential to that particular feedback topology**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Cause – The Three-Level Path Criterion

Let the asynchronous circuit have:
- Primary input $x$
- Secondary (feedback) variable $y$
- Excitation function $Y = f(x, y)$ feeding a delay element whose output is $y$

A **single change in $x$** propagates to the input of the delay element (which produces $y$) along multiple paths inside the combinational cloud. If $x$ reaches the same feedback gate through an **odd number of inversions** on one path and an **even number of inversions** on another, then for a brief interval the **feedback variable is driven in the wrong direction**, creating an essential hazard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the input change must pass through **three or more levels of logic** before reaching a feedback variable, the circuit is a *prime suspect* for essential hazards. This is precisely why textbook designs try to keep the feedback loop shallow.

## 2.2 State-Table Criterion (Most Exam-Relevant)

Given a state transition table $S \times X \rightarrow S$:

> An **essential hazard exists** if, for some present state $s_i$ and some input transition $x_j \rightarrow x_k$ (where $x_j \neq x_k$), the **stable destination state** is $s_f$, but the **first unstable entry** in column $x_k$ of row $s_i$ points to a state **different from $s_f$**.

In simpler words: after a single input change, the circuit "jumps" through one or more **unstable intermediate states** before returning to the *correct* stable state. If the jump involves an odd number of intermediate transitions (often 1, but the diagnostic signature is the inconsistency with the steady-state expectation), an essential hazard is present.

## 2.3 Detection Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. Identify all **stable states** in the merged/primitive flow table.
2. For each stable state, list the **single input transitions** that change its column.
3. Verify the **expected next stable state** based on the machine's intended flow.
4. Check whether the **unstable entry** in the new column immediately agrees with the expected stable state.
5. If not, trace whether a *single additional* transition (induced by the feedback variable) would land on the correct state — this confirms an essential hazard rather than a static/dynamic hazard.
6. Count the number of logic levels from the changed input back to the feedback variable; if $\geq 3$, **essential hazard is confirmed**.

## 2.4 Elimination Strategy

- **Delay Equalization:** Insert precisely tuned delay elements (buffers, gate delays) on the *shorter* path so that all feedback paths have matched propagation delay.
- **Structural Redesign:** Re-route the feedback so that the changed input reaches the feedback variable through **uniform delay**.
- **Two-Level Realization:** Convert multi-level combinational excitation logic into a 2-level SOP/POS form *where feasible*, then verify path lengths.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning / Condition | Exam Note |
|---|---|---|
| $Y$ | Next-state (excitation) function | Function of $x$ and feedback $y$ |
| $y$ | Present state (delay output) | Updates only after propagation delay |
| $t_{pd,\min}$ | Shortest propagation delay from $x$ to feedback gate | Used for delay equalization |
| $t_{pd,\max}$ | Longest propagation delay from $x$ to feedback gate | Determines hazard window |
| $\Delta t = t_{pd,\max} - t_{pd,\min}$ | Hazard window | If $\Delta t > 0$, essential hazard *possible* |
| $\#\text{levels}(x \to y) \geq 3$ | Sufficient structural condition | Mandatory check in KTU answers |
| Stable state condition | $Y = y$ for the prevailing input column | Underline entries in flow table |
| Essential hazard signature | Single input change $\Rightarrow$ 3+ output transitions | Before settling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars inside formula cells.** When writing expressions like $\vert Y - y \vert$ or $\vert \Delta t \vert$, use the LaTeX vertical bar command $\vert$ inside math mode, NOT the literal pipe character, otherwise the markdown table will break.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Essential hazards are the primary reason why **asynchronous micro-pipelines, NULL Convention Logic (NCL) circuits, and self-timed ARMs (e.g., ARM996HS)** require meticulous delay-matching across handshake lines. In safety-critical asynchronous designs (aerospace, automotive, medical implants), essential hazards are formally verified using **model checkers** (e.g., NuSMV, ABC) and trace-based timing analysis. Mastery of essential hazards is therefore not merely academic — it directly governs the reliability of every clockless VLSI system you will encounter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

Consider an asynchronous sequential circuit with:
- One primary input $x$
- One feedback variable $y$
- Excitation function $Y = x \cdot y + \bar{x} \cdot \bar{y}$ (this is the XOR of $x$ and $y$)

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Derive the State Table

We construct the next-state values for each combination of $(y, x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x,\, y) = (0,\, 0) &\Rightarrow Y = 0\cdot 0 + \bar{0}\cdot \bar{0} = 0 + 1\cdot 1 = 1 \\[4pt]
(x,\, y) = (0,\, 1) &\Rightarrow Y = 0\cdot 1 + \bar{0}\cdot \bar{1} = 0 + 1\cdot 0 = 0 \\[4pt]
(x,\, y) = (1,\, 0) &\Rightarrow Y = 1\cdot 0 + \bar{1}\cdot \bar{0} = 0 + 0\cdot 1 = 0 \\[4pt]
(x,\, y) = (1,\, 1) &\Rightarrow Y = 1\cdot 1 + \bar{1}\cdot \bar{1} = 1 + 0\cdot 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting State Transition Table:**

| Present $y$ | $Y$ for $x=0$ | $Y$ for $x=1$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 1 |

Stable states (underlined) are those where $Y = y$:

| Present $y$ | $x=0$ | $x=1$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | **1** | **0** |
| 1 | **0** | **1** |

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Trace a Single Input Change

Suppose the circuit is in stable state $y=0$ with $x=0$. Then $x$ changes $0 \rightarrow 1$.

- **Expected behavior:** $y$ should remain at $0$ (because $Y=0$ for $(x=1, y=0)$).
- **Actual transient trace (with unequal delays):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = t_0 &\Rightarrow x \text{ becomes } 1 \\
t = t_0 + 2\,\tau &\Rightarrow \bar{x}\text{-path} \text{ sees the change first (short path)}\\
&\Rightarrow \text{term } \bar{x}\cdot \bar{y} \text{ drives } Y = 0 \cdot 1 = 0\\
&\Rightarrow y \text{ remains } 0 \quad \text{(coincidentally correct, but for wrong reason)}\\
t = t_0 + 5\,\tau &\Rightarrow x\text{-path} \text{ finally settles, but the AND-gate } x\cdot y \text{ briefly sees } x=1, y=0\\
&\Rightarrow Y = 0 \quad \text{(now stable and consistent)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — A Clearer Essential-Hazard Demonstration

Now consider $Y = x \cdot \bar{y} + \bar{x} \cdot y$ (XNOR of $x$ and $y$), implemented as two cascaded gates with asymmetric delays:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x,\, y) = (0,\, 0) &\Rightarrow Y = 0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 0 = 0 \quad \text{(stable)}\\
(x,\, y) = (0,\, 1) &\Rightarrow Y = 0\cdot 0 + 1\cdot 1 = 1 \quad \text{(stable)}\\
(x,\, y) = (1,\, 0) &\Rightarrow Y = 1\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 0 = 1 \quad \text{(stable)}\\
(x,\, y) = (1,\, 1) &\Rightarrow Y = 1\cdot 0 + 0\cdot 1 = 0 \quad \text{(stable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Suppose $y=0, x=0$ (stable), and $x$ switches to $1$.

- **Steady-state expectation:** $y$ should transition to $1$.
- **Trace through three logic levels (input $\to$ gate A $\to$ gate B $\to$ feedback gate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path 1 (short):} &\quad x \xrightarrow{\text{NOT}} \bar{x}=0 \xrightarrow{\text{AND}} \bar{x}\cdot y = 0 \quad \text{(arrives early)}\\
\text{Path 2 (long):} &\quad x \xrightarrow{\text{AND}} x\cdot \bar{y} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \quad \text{(arrives late)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a brief window between the two arrivals, the OR-gate output $Y$ sees:
- Term 1 = 0 (from Path 1, already updated)
- Term 2 = 1 (from Path 2, *not yet* updated — still using old $x=0$, so $x\cdot \bar{y}=0$)

This gives $Y=0$ momentarily, then $Y=1$. The feedback variable therefore **oscillates or goes through an erroneous intermediate state**, exactly satisfying the essential-hazard definition.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Confirm via the Three-Level Rule

Counting logic levels from the changed input $x$ back to the feedback variable $y$:

$$
x \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AND}_1 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{OR} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AND}_2 \;(\text{feedback gate})
$$

That is **3 levels**, confirming the structural rule.

## 3.2 Elimination Derivation

To eliminate the hazard, we insert a delay buffer of $\Delta = t_{pd,\text{long}} - t_{pd,\text{short}}$ on Path 1:

$$
t'_{pd,\text{short}} = t_{pd,\text{short}} + \Delta = t_{pd,\text{long}}
$$

After equalization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t'_1 &= t_{pd,\text{short}} + \Delta\\
t'_2 &= t_{pd,\text{long}}\\
\Rightarrow t'_1 &= t'_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The two transitions now arrive **simultaneously** at the OR gate, so no spurious intermediate $Y$ value occurs. The hazard window collapses to zero.

## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Step | What We Do | What We Conclude |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Build next-state table from excitation $Y$ | Identifies stable vs unstable states |
| 2 | Trace single input change from each stable state | Reveals if steady-state destination is correct |
| 3 | Count logic levels from $x$ to feedback $y$ | $\geq 3$ levels $\Rightarrow$ essential hazard likely |
| 4 | Compute $\Delta t = t_{pd,\max} - t_{pd,\min}$ | Quantifies hazard severity |
| 5 | Insert delay buffer of magnitude $\Delta t$ | Eliminates hazard structurally |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of an Essential-Hazard-Prone Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram traces how a single input change propagates back to the feedback variable through two paths of unequal length — the geometric origin of essential hazards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Primary Input x] --> pthA{Path A: Short}
    src --> pthB{Path B: Long}
    pthA --> gateA[AND Gate A]
    pthB --> gateB[AND Gate B]
    gateA --> orGate[OR Gate producing Y]
    gateB --> orGate
    orGate --> delay[Propagation Delay Element]
    delay --> fb[Feedback Variable y]
    fb --> gateA
    fb --> gateB
    style src fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style gateA fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style gateB fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style orGate fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style delay fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style fb fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The same input `x` enters both AND gates through paths of unequal length. Their outputs are OR-ed to form the excitation `Y`, which passes through a delay element before becoming the feedback `y`. The asymmetry between Path A and Path B is precisely what enables the essential hazard.

## 4.2 Timing Diagram Showing the Hazard Window

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant X as Input x
    participant PA as Path A Output
    participant PB as Path B Output
    participant Y as Y Output
    participant Yvar as Feedback y

    X->>PA: t = t0 (change arrives fast)
    PA->>Y: t = t0 + 2tau (term1 settles)
    X->>PB: t = t0 (change en route long path)
    PB->>Y: t = t0 + 5tau (term2 finally settles)
    Note over Y: t in [t0+2tau, t0+5tau] => WRONG intermediate value
    Y->>Yvar: incorrect y briefly latched
    Note over Yvar: ESSENTIAL HAZARD WINDOW
    Y->>Yvar: t = t0 + 5tau (correct y finally latched)
```

## 4.3 State-Transition Flow Showing the Erroneous Intermediate

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0
    S0 --> S0: x stays at 0 (stable)
    S0 --> Sinter: x changes 0 to 1 (transient)
    Sinter --> Sfinal: feedback settles late
    Sfinal --> Sfinal: correct stable destination
    note right of Sinter: ESSENTIAL HAZARD visible here
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The intermediate state `Sinter` exists only because of unequal path delays. In a *delay-equalized* design, `Sinter` collapses and the transition becomes a direct edge `S0 --> Sfinal`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q1.** Define an **essential hazard**. Why is it called *essential* and why can it not be removed by adding redundant prime implicants?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **essential hazard** is a timing fault occurring in asynchronous sequential circuits where a single change in a primary input causes the secondary (feedback) variables to pass through an **unwanted intermediate state** before settling into the correct stable state. It arises due to **unequal propagation delays** in the feedback loop when the changed input reaches a feedback variable through paths of different lengths.

It is called *essential* because the hazard is **intrinsic to the feedback topology** of that particular asynchronous circuit; any equivalent Boolean realization of the same excitation function will exhibit the same hazard as long as the feedback structure is preserved. Adding redundant prime implicants — a technique used to suppress static hazards in combinational circuits — has **no effect** because the fault is in the *temporal* path-length asymmetry, not in the *spatial* Boolean coverage. The only cure is **delay equalization** of the feedback paths.

*(Examiner's break-up: 1 mark definition, 1 mark "why essential", 1 mark "why redundant terms fail")*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q2.** State the **three-level rule** for detecting essential hazards from a circuit schematic. Why is the count $\geq 3$ critical?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **three-level rule** states: *if a single change in any primary input must propagate through three or more levels of combinational logic before reaching a feedback variable that influences the very path being excited, the circuit contains an essential hazard.*

The threshold of **three** is critical because, with only two levels, all paths from the input to the feedback gate are of equal logical depth; any single change arrives coherently. Once the depth reaches three, however, the changed signal can reach the same feedback gate along paths with differing numbers of inversions — causing a momentary drive in the wrong direction and producing the spurious intermediate state that defines the essential hazard.

*(Examiner's break-up: 1 mark statement of rule, 1 mark reason for $\geq 3$, 1 mark connection to unequal inversions)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

> **Q (A). (a)** For an asynchronous sequential circuit with one input $x$ and one feedback variable $y$, the excitation function is $Y = x\bar{y} + \bar{x}y$. Draw the **state table**, identify all stable states, and **determine whether an essential hazard exists**. Justify with the three-level rule.
>
> **(b)** If the hazard exists, propose a **delay-equalization strategy** and re-derive the corrected transition trace showing the elimination of the erroneous intermediate state.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Build the state table** [2 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(0,0) &= 0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 0 = 0\\
Y(0,1) &= 0\cdot 0 + 1\cdot 1 = 1\\
Y(1,0) &= 1\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 0 = 1\\
Y(1,1) &= 1\cdot 0 + 0\cdot 1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

| Present $y$ | $x=0$ | $x=1$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | **0** | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | **0** |

**Step 2 — Mark stable states (bold/underlined entries)** [1 mark]

Stable states occur where $Y = y$: $(y=0, x=0)$ and $(y=1, x=1)$.

**Step 3 — Apply the three-level rule** [2 marks]

Tracing from input $x$ to the feedback gate that produces $y$:

$$
x \;\to\; \text{NOT} \;\to\; \text{AND}_1 \;(x\bar{y}) \;\to\; \text{OR} \;\to\; \text{feedback-gate}
$$

That is **3 levels** of combinational logic; therefore by the three-level rule the circuit **contains an essential hazard**.

**Step 4 — Confirm via transient trace** [2 marks]

Suppose $y=0, x=0$ (stable). Let $x$ switch to $1$:
- Through the $\bar{x}y$ path (short), $\bar{x}$ becomes $0$ first, so the term $\bar{x}y$ drops to $0$.
- Through the $x\bar{y}$ path (long), the AND-gate output takes longer to update, so for a brief window the OR gate sees both terms evaluating against *inconsistent* $x$ values.
- This produces an erroneous transient $Y = 0$ before the correct $Y = 1$ settles, causing $y$ to dip through an illegal intermediate — **confirming essential hazard**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Quantify the asymmetry** [2 marks]

Let $t_{pd,A}$ be the delay through the $\bar{x}y$ path and $t_{pd,B}$ be the delay through the $x\bar{y}$ path.

$$
\Delta t = t_{pd,B} - t_{pd,A} > 0
$$

**Step 2 — Insert delay buffer on Path A** [2 marks]

Introduce a buffer of delay $\Delta t$ on the short path so that:

$$
t'_{pd,A} = t_{pd,A} + \Delta t = t_{pd,B}
$$

**Step 3 — Re-derive the corrected trace** [2 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = t_0 &\Rightarrow x \text{ becomes } 1\\
t = t_0 + t'_{pd,A} &\Rightarrow \text{both } x\bar{y} \text{ and } \bar{x}y \text{ update simultaneously}\\
Y &\Rightarrow 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 = 1\\
y &\Rightarrow 1 \quad \text{(direct, no intermediate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion** [1 mark]

The hazardous intermediate state is **eliminated**, and the transition becomes a clean $y:0 \rightarrow y:1$.

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Module Choice]

> **Q (B). (a)** Explain with a suitable example how an **essential hazard is identified from the state table** of an asynchronous sequential circuit. Use a circuit with input $x$ and feedback $y$, where $Y = xy + \bar{x}\bar{y}$.
>
> **(b)** Compare essential hazards with **static-1 and dynamic hazards** in terms of (i) domain of occurrence, (ii) root cause, and (iii) method of elimination.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Derive the state table** [2 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(0,0) &= 0\cdot 0 + 1\cdot 1 = 1\\
Y(0,1) &= 0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 0 = 1\\
Y(1,0) &= 1\cdot 0 + 0\cdot 1 = 0\\
Y(1,1) &= 1\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

| Present $y$ | $x=0$ | $x=1$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | **0** |
| 1 | 1 | **1** |

**Step 2 — Identify stable states** [1 mark]

$(y=0, x=1)$ and $(y=1, x=1)$ are stable because $Y=y$ there.

**Step 3 — Trace input change** [2 marks]

Start at stable state $y=0, x=1$. Switch $x$ from $1$ to $0$.
- Expected new column: $x=0$. The unstable entry for $y=0, x=0$ is $Y=1$, meaning the circuit must transition to $y=1$.
- Expected destination: stable $y=1, x=0$.

**Step 4 — Identify the essential hazard** [2 marks]

Counting logic levels from $x$ back to the feedback gate gives **3 levels** ($x \to$ AND $\to$ OR $\to$ feedback gate). Therefore, between the moment $x$ changes and the moment $y$ updates, an erroneous transient value of $Y$ appears, creating an essential hazard. The state table signature: the unstable entry in the new column does *not* match the immediate expected destination when path delays are unequal.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table** [5 marks for full table; partial credit by row]

| Aspect | Static-1 Hazard | Dynamic Hazard | Essential Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Domain | Combinational | Multi-level combinational | Asynchronous sequential (with feedback) |
| (ii) Root Cause | A changing input briefly disables both AND terms of an OR-based SOP, momentarily dropping output from 1 to 0 | A changing input traverses multiple paths, causing output to oscillate 0$\to$1$\to$0 before settling | Single input change reaches feedback variable through unequal-delay paths |
| (iii) Elimination | Add the consensus/redundant prime implicant | Redesign as 2-level SOP/POS to equalize paths | Insert matched delay buffers on shorter paths; redesign feedback routing |

**Concluding remark** [2 marks]:

Static hazards are *spatial* (Boolean-coverage) defects; dynamic hazards are *transitional* defects; essential hazards are *temporal-architectural* defects. The cures are therefore orthogonal: logic minimization, restructuring, and delay engineering — applied together give the most robust asynchronous design.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating static hazards with essential hazards** — students frequently write "essential hazard can be removed by adding a redundant term" — this is a *static-hazard* cure and is **completely wrong** for essential hazards. Marks deducted: typically 2 out of 3.
> 2. **Omitting the "asynchronous sequential" qualifier** in the definition — the examiner checks this phrase first. Marks deducted: 1.
> 3. **Forgetting to underline stable states** in the state table. Marks deducted: 1.
> 4. **Writing $\vert Y - y \vert$ with the literal pipe character** — this breaks the markdown table rendering in the digital answer script and confuses the evaluator. Always write $\vert Y - y \vert$ in math mode.
> 5. **Missing the delay-equalization step** in elimination questions — partial credit only if path delay analysis is explicitly shown.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Essential hazards occur **only in asynchronous sequential circuits** with feedback — never in combinational or synchronous designs.
- They are caused by **unequal propagation delays** along feedback paths when a single input change reaches a feedback variable through different routes.
- The **three-level rule** is the fastest detection shortcut: count the combinational levels from the changing input back to the feedback gate; $\geq 3$ ⇒ suspect essential hazard.
- They are **NOT eliminated by Boolean redundancy / consensus terms** — these fixes apply to static hazards.
- The only structural cure is **delay equalization** (matched buffers) or **feedback re-routing**.
- In KTU answers, always: (i) **define** the hazard, (ii) **show the state table**, (iii) **underline stable entries**, (iv) **trace the transient**, (v) **propose the delay fix**.
- Use $\vert \Delta t \vert$, $\vert Y \vert$, etc., with the LaTeX $\vert$ command — never the literal pipe inside a markdown table.
- Real-world relevance: clockless VLSI, NULL Convention Logic (NCL), self-timed pipelines, ARM996HS, and safety-critical asynchronous SoCs all rely on essential-hazard-free design.
- **Memorize** the difference between essential, static, and dynamic hazards — KTU almost always asks a direct 3-mark comparison in either Part A or as a Part B sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Hazard Free Circuits
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Design of Hazard-Free Circuits

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT402 - DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Hazard Free Circuits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Hazard** is an unwanted, momentary spurious pulse (glitch) at the output of a combinational logic circuit that occurs due to unequal propagation delays through different gate paths when an input variable changes its value, even though the Boolean function (steady-state output) remains the same. A **Hazard-Free Circuit** is one in which no such transient glitches appear at the output for any single input variable transition.

### 1.1 Classification of Hazards (As per KTU Syllabus)

KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes hazards into **three** distinct types based on the steady-state and dynamic behavior of the output:

| Hazard Type | Steady-State Output | Transient Behavior | KTU Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Static-1 Hazard** | Should remain at logic **1** | Briefly glitches to **0**, then back to **1** | $S_1$ |
| **Static-0 Hazard** | Should remain at logic **0** | Briefly glitches to **1**, then back to **0** | $S_0$ |
| **Dynamic Hazard** | Should transition from **0 → 1** (or 1 → 0) | Output oscillates multiple times before settling | $D$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** KTU strictly tests **Static Hazards** (both $S_1$ and $S_0$) and **Dynamic Hazards** in two-level AND-OR / OR-AND networks. Multi-level circuits are also discussed in the context of dynamic hazards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a **traffic signal** that should logically remain **GREEN** forever. Due to a faulty electrical wire, the signal momentarily flickers to **YELLOW** and then back to **GREEN** before you can even cross the road. 

In a digital circuit:
- The **Green Light** = Correct Boolean Output (e.g., $F = 1$).
- The **Yellow Flicker** = Unwanted glitch (a momentary $0$ pulse).
- The **Faulty Wire** = Different propagation delays in two adjacent AND/OR gates feeding the same output.

Even though the *final* answer (Green = 1) is correct, the *transient* flicker could be interpreted by a downstream flip-flop as a valid clock edge or a valid data pulse, causing catastrophic **system-level errors** in synchronous digital systems (e.g., counters, registers, memory addressing).

### 1.3 Why Hazards Matter in Real Engineering

- **Asynchronous Sequential Circuits**: Hazards cause incorrect state transitions (race conditions).
- **Synchronous Systems**: If a hazard occurs near the active clock edge, it can be **latched** into a flip-flop, corrupting stored data.
- **High-Speed CMOS Designs**: Propagation delay mismatches are amplified at GHz clock frequencies, making hazard-free design **mandatory** in VLSI design flows.
- **Standard Metric**: The **propagation delay** of the slowest gate path ($t_{pd,max}$) is typically measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**, and any path mismatch greater than this window can cause a hazard.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cause of Hazards — The Race Condition

A hazard arises due to a **logic race** between two or more signal paths converging at a single gate. When an input variable $x$ transitions from one value to another, the signals travelling through different gate paths reach the converging gate at **different times**, producing a momentary incorrect output.

Consider a 2-level SOP circuit implementing:
$$F = AB + \bar{A}C$$

When $B = 1$ and $C = 1$, the function reduces to $F = A + \bar{A}$, which is *logically* equal to $1$. However, in a real circuit:

- Path 1: $\bar{A}$ → AND gate → OR gate.
- Path 2: $A$ → AND gate → OR gate.

If the **inverter** has a finite delay ($\Delta t$), then for a brief moment, both $A$ and $\bar{A}$ may be at $0$ when $A$ switches from $1 \to 0$. The OR gate output momentarily drops to $0$, producing a **Static-1 Hazard**.

## 2.2 Static-1 Hazard in SOP Form (Two-Level AND-OR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> A two-level AND-OR (SOP) circuit is **free of static-1 hazards** if and only if it contains the **consensus term** (redundant prime implicant) for every pair of adjacent (logically adjacent) minterms that differ by exactly one variable.

The consensus term of two minterms $m_i$ and $m_j$ differing in variable $x$ is the product of all literals common to both minterms (excluding the differing variable).

## 2.3 Static-0 Hazard in POS Form (Two-Level OR-AND)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duality Theorem for POS:**
> A two-level OR-AND (POS) circuit is **free of static-0 hazards** if and only if it contains the **consensus of maxterms** (redundant prime implicate) for every pair of adjacent maxterms that differ by exactly one variable. The consensus here is the **sum** of common literals.

## 2.4 Dynamic Hazard Condition

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** A dynamic hazard can occur **only** in **multi-level (3 or more levels)** non-associative logic networks. A two-level (SOP/POS) circuit **cannot** exhibit dynamic hazards by definition. This is a frequent exam trap question.

A dynamic hazard occurs when the output has **multiple transient changes** (e.g., $0 \to 1 \to 0 \to 1$) before settling to its final value, during a single input transition that should produce exactly one output change.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Condition | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Boolean Output (SOP Reduced) | $F = AB + \bar{A}C$ | Generates $1$-hazard when $B=C=1$ |
| 2 | Boolean Output (POS Reduced) | $F = (A + B)(\bar{A} + C)$ | Generates $0$-hazard when $B=C=0$ |
| 3 | Hazard-Free SOP Form | $F = AB + \bar{A}C + BC$ | $BC$ is the **consensus/redundant term** |
| 4 | Hazard-Free POS Form | $F = (A+B)(\bar{A}+C)(B+C)$ | $(B+C)$ is the **consensus maxterm** |
| 5 | Adjacency Test | Minterms $m_i$, $m_j$ differ in 1 bit | $d(m_i, m_j) = 1$ (Hamming Distance) |
| 6 | Consensus Term (SOP) | $x \cdot y + \bar{x} \cdot z + yz$ | $yz$ eliminates $x$-transition hazard |
| 7 | Static Hazard Window | $t_{hazard} \leq 2 \cdot t_{pd,gate}$ | Window in **ns** or **ps** |
| 8 | Dynamic Hazard Requirement | $\geq 3$ logic levels | Two-level circuits are immune |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** The hazard pulse width $t_{w}$ is bounded by the propagation delay of the slowest gate in the race path. If $t_w < t_{setup}$ of the destination flip-flop, the hazard is **transparent** (harmless); otherwise, it is **latched** (catastrophic).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hazard-free design is critical in:
- **Asynchronous Arbiters & Handshake Circuits** (AMBA AXI bus).
- **Decoder Circuits** in memory address selection.
- **Glitch-Free Clock Trees** in synchronous ASICs.
- **Safety-Critical Systems**: Aerospace flight controllers, automotive ECUs (ISO 26262), and medical devices (IEC 62304) mandate hazard-free combinational logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hazard in $F = AB + \bar{A}C$

### Step 1 — Define the Function and the Test Conditions
Let the target function be:
$$F(A, B, C) = A \cdot B + \bar{A} \cdot C$$

Assume we hold $B = 1$ and $C = 1$ as constant control inputs. Then:
$$F = A(1) + \bar{A}(1) = A + \bar{A}$$

### Step 2 — Logical (Algebraic) Simplification
By Boolean algebra, $A + \bar{A} = 1$. So the **steady-state output** for any value of $A$ should be logic $1$. There is no logical reason for the output to ever go low.

### Step 3 — Physical (Gate-Level) Realization
In hardware, the circuit is implemented as two AND gates and one OR gate:
- **AND1** computes $A \cdot B$.
- **AND2** computes $\bar{A} \cdot C$ (the $\bar{A}$ requires a NOT gate).
- **OR** computes $F = (A \cdot B) + (\bar{A} \cdot C)$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Critical Input Transition
Let the input $A$ transition from $1 \to 0$ at time $t = 0$. Let the inverter delay be $t_{inv} = 5\,\text{ns}$ and the AND/OR gate delay be $t_{and} = t_{or} = 5\,\text{ns}$.

| Time $t$ (ns) | $A$ | $\bar{A}$ | $A \cdot B$ | $\bar{A} \cdot C$ | $F$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $0^-$ (before) | $1$ | $0$ | $1 \cdot 1 = 1$ | $0 \cdot 1 = 0$ | $1$ |
| $0$ (just after) | $0$ | $0$ (still) | $0 \cdot 1 = 0$ | $0 \cdot 1 = 0$ | $\mathbf{0}$ ← **GLITCH** |
| $5$ (after inverter) | $0$ | $1$ | $0 \cdot 1 = 0$ | $1 \cdot 1 = 1$ | $1$ |

The output $F$ momentarily drops to $0$ between $t = 0$ and $t = 5\,\text{ns}$ — this is a **Static-1 Hazard** with pulse width $t_w = 5\,\text{ns}$.

### Step 5 — Formal Identification using Boolean Identity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reduced form $F = AB + \bar{A}C$ is **NOT** hazard-free because the term $AB$ requires $A=1$ to be active, while $\bar{A}C$ requires $A=0$ to be active. There is **no term** that guarantees $F=1$ during the $A$-transition when $B=C=1$.

The consensus of $AB$ and $\bar{A}C$ (with respect to $A$) is the term:
$$(B) \cdot (C) = BC$$

Adding this consensus term to the function:
$$F_{HF} = AB + \bar{A}C + BC$$

### Step 6 — Prove Hazard-Free Behavior Algebraically
For the critical transition condition $B = C = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{HF} &= A(1) + \bar{A}(1) + (1)(1) \\
&= A + \bar{A} + 1 \\
&= 1 + 1 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}$$

The redundant term $BC$ is constantly $1$ when $B=C=1$, so it **covers** the transition window of $A$, keeping the output firmly at $1$ regardless of the inverter delay.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Minterms to Hazard Coverage

For a 3-variable K-map with $A$ as the transitioning variable and $B, C$ as the control variables, the four minterms are:

| Minterm | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | Covered by Term |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $m_3$ | $0$ | $1$ | $1$ | $\bar{A}C$ |
| $m_7$ | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | $AB$ |
| $m_3$ ↔ $m_7$ (Adjacent) | — | — | — | **$BC$ (consensus bridge)** |

The consensus term $BC$ physically bridges the two minterms $m_3$ and $m_7$ that lie **adjacent** on the K-map (differ in $A$ only). This bridge ensures no gap exists during the $A$-transition.

## 3.3 POS Derivation: Static-0 Hazard in $F = (A+B)(\bar{A}+C)$

### Step 1 — Reduce under Test Condition $B = 0$, $C = 0$
$$F = (A+0)(\bar{A}+0) = A \cdot \bar{A}$$

Logically, $A \cdot \bar{A} = 0$, so output should stay at $0$.

### Step 2 — Physical Realization (OR-AND)
- **OR1** computes $A+B$.
- **OR2** computes $\bar{A}+C$ (with inverter on $A$).
- **AND** computes $F = (A+B)(\bar{A}+C)$.

### Step 3 — Transition Analysis ($A: 0 \to 1$)
At the moment $A$ switches, both $A+B = 0+0 = 0$ and $\bar{A}+C = 0+0 = 0$ momentarily, causing $F$ to glitch **up to 1** → **Static-0 Hazard**.

### Step 4 — Add Consensus Maxterm
The consensus of $A+B$ and $\bar{A}+C$ (w.r.t. $A$) is:
$$B + C$$

The hazard-free POS expression is:
$$F_{HF} = (A+B)(\bar{A}+C)(B+C)$$

### Step 5 — Verification
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{HF} &= (A+0)(\bar{A}+0)(0+0) \\
&= (A)(\bar{A})(0) \\
&= 0 \cdot 0 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

The term $(B+C) = 0$ forces $F=0$ during the entire transition window, eliminating the glitch.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hazard Detection Flow in Combinational Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Input Vector with Transition A] --> p1[Path 1: Direct AND gate]
    startA --> inv[Inverter producing A-bar]
    inv --> p2[Path 2: Inverted AND gate]
    p1 --> conv[Converging OR gate]
    p2 --> conv
    conv --> outA[Output F with Glitch]
    startB[Hazard Detection Module] --> kmap[Map minterms on K-map]
    kmap --> adj[Identify Adjacent Minterms differing in 1 variable]
    adj --> cons[Compute Consensus Term]
    cons --> add[Add Redundant Consensus to F]
    add --> fhf[Hazard-Free Function F-hf]
    fhf --> outB[Glitch-Free Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Circuit Schematic: AND-OR Network with Redundant Consensus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Input A] --> a1[AND gate AB]
    inpB[Input B] --> a1
    inpA --> inv1[Inverter NOT]
    inv1 --> a2[AND gate A-bar C]
    inpC[Input C] --> a2
    inpB --> a3[AND gate BC]
    inpC --> a3
    a1 --> or1[OR gate final]
    a2 --> or1
    a3 --> or1
    or1 --> outF[Output F = AB + A-bar C + BC]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid K-Map Cell Diagram (3-Variable)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    m0[Cell m0: A-bar B-bar C-bar = 0]
    m1[Cell m1: A-bar B-bar C = 0]
    m2[Cell m2: A-bar B C = 1]
    m3[Cell m3: A-bar B C = 1]
    m4[Cell m4: A B-bar C-bar = 0]
    m5[Cell m5: A B-bar C = 0]
    m6[Cell m6: A B C-bar = 1]
    m7[Cell m7: A B C = 1]
    m2 --- m3
    m3 --- m7
    m7 --- m6
    m6 --- m2
    grp1[Group 1: m3+m7 = BC consensus]
    grp2[Group 2: m2+m3 = A-bar C]
    grp3[Group 3: m6+m7 = AB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the K-Map:** The four 1-cells form a $2 \times 2$ block. Three prime implicants exist: $A\bar{C}$, $BC$, and $AB$. The minimal SOP form $F = AB + \bar{A}C$ omits the consensus $BC$, which causes a hazard. The hazard-free form $F = AB + \bar{A}C + BC$ adds this consensus.

## 4.4 Mermaid Hazard Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Input Change Occurs] --> q1{Output should stay constant?}
    q1 -- Yes, output 1 --> s1[Static-1 Hazard: F=1->0->1]
    q1 -- Yes, output 0 --> s0[Static-0 Hazard: F=0->1->0]
    q1 -- No, should change --> q2{More than one transient change?}
    q2 -- Yes --> dyn[Dynamic Hazard: multi-level only]
    q2 -- No --> safe[Clean Transition: no hazard]
    s1 --> fix1[Add SOP consensus term]
    s0 --> fix2[Add POS consensus term]
    dyn --> fix3[Use multi-level hazard-free design]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a hazard in a digital combinational circuit. Differentiate between static and dynamic hazards.**

**Model Answer:**
A **hazard** is an unwanted transient pulse appearing at the output of a combinational circuit due to unequal propagation delays, even though the Boolean function remains logically correct.

**Static Hazard** — The output is *supposed* to remain constant (either 0 or 1) but glitches to the opposite value momentarily. It is of two types:
- **Static-1 Hazard** ($S_1$): Output should stay at 1 but glitches to 0.
- **Static-0 Hazard** ($S_0$): Output should stay at 0 but glitches to 1.

**Dynamic Hazard** — The output is supposed to change state (e.g., 0→1) but undergoes **multiple** unwanted transitions before settling to its final value. Dynamic hazards occur **only in multi-level (≥3 level) circuits** and cannot exist in two-level SOP/POS networks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a redundant prime implicant? How does it help in eliminating static-1 hazards?**

**Model Answer:**
A **redundant prime implicant** (also called a *consensus term*) is a prime implicant of a Boolean function that covers minterms already covered by other prime implicants, and is therefore *not strictly necessary* for minimal SOP realization. However, it covers **logically adjacent** minterms that differ in exactly one variable.

When a single input variable transitions, the output must remain constant for all values of the other variables. A minimal SOP form may leave a **gap** in coverage during the transition window. By adding the consensus term that bridges adjacent minterms (e.g., adding $BC$ to $F = AB + \bar{A}C$), the output is forced to remain at the correct logic level throughout the transition, thereby **eliminating the static-1 hazard**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Implement the Boolean function $F(A, B, C, D) = \sum m(0, 2, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 13, 14)$ using a hazard-free two-level AND-OR gate network. Show the K-map, identify all hazards, and add the necessary redundant terms to eliminate them.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A combinational circuit is described by the function $F = AB + \bar{B}C$. Determine the type of hazard present, and design a hazard-free circuit. Draw the gate-level schematic.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — K-Map Realization

Placing the minterms on a 4-variable K-map with $AB$ as rows and $CD$ as columns:

| AB\CD | 00 | 01 | 11 | 10 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **00** | 1 ($m_0$) | 0 | 0 | 1 ($m_2$) |
| **01** | 1 ($m_4$) | 1 ($m_5$) | 0 | 1 ($m_6$) |
| **11** | 1 ($m_{12}$) | 1 ($m_{13}$) | 0 | 1 ($m_{14}$) |
| **10** | 1 ($m_8$) | 0 | 0 | 1 ($m_{10}$) |

**Minimal SOP (Hazard-Prone):**
$$F = \bar{A}\bar{D} + A\bar{D} + B\bar{C} + \bar{B}\bar{D}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{min} &= \bar{D}(\bar{A} + A) + B\bar{C} + \bar{B}\bar{D} \\
&= \bar{D} + B\bar{C} + \bar{B}\bar{D}
\end{aligned}$$

**Hazard Identification:**
- Transition of $A$ when $B=0, C=0, D=0$: covers $m_0$ and $m_4$ (adjacent) → needs consensus $\bar{B}\bar{C}\bar{D}$ to prevent $A$-transition glitch.
- Transition of $B$ when $A=0, C=0, D=0$: covers $m_0$ and $m_2$ → needs consensus $\bar{A}\bar{C}\bar{D}$.
- Transition of $B$ when $A=1, C=0, D=0$: covers $m_8$ and $m_{10}$ → needs consensus $A\bar{C}\bar{D}$.

**Hazard-Free SOP:**
$$F_{HF} = \bar{D} + B\bar{C} + \bar{B}\bar{D} + \bar{A}\bar{C}\bar{D} + A\bar{C}\bar{D}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{HF} &= \bar{D} + B\bar{C} + \bar{B}\bar{D} + \bar{C}\bar{D}(\bar{A}+A) \\
&= \bar{D} + B\bar{C} + \bar{B}\bar{D} + \bar{C}\bar{D}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\bar{D}$ already covers $\bar{C}\bar{D}$, the additional consensus $\bar{C}\bar{D}$ is redundant, but the consensus terms $\bar{B}\bar{C}\bar{D}$ and $A\bar{C}\bar{D}$ are essential for full hazard coverage.

#### Part (b) — Analysis of $F = AB + \bar{B}C$

Setting $A = 1$ and $C = 1$ as test conditions:
$$F = B + \bar{B} = 1 \quad \text{(logically)}$$

When $B$ transitions from $1 \to 0$, the inverter delay causes a brief window where both $B = 0$ and $\bar{B} = 0$, making $F$ momentarily drop to $0$. This is a **Static-1 Hazard**.

**Consensus Term** of $AB$ and $\bar{B}C$ with respect to $B$ is $AC$.

**Hazard-Free Form:**
$$F_{HF} = AB + \bar{B}C + AC$$

**Verification** at $A=1, C=1$:
$$F_{HF} = (1)(1) + 0 + (1)(1) = 1 + 0 + 1 = 1 \quad \text{(Glitch-Free)}$$

**Gate Schematic (described):** Three 2-input AND gates (for $AB$, $\bar{B}C$, $AC$) feeding a 3-input OR gate, with a single NOT gate producing $\bar{B}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a hazard-free circuit for $F = (A+C)(\bar{A}+B)$ in POS form. Identify the static-0 hazard and add the consensus maxterm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain how dynamic hazards occur in multi-level circuits. Why are two-level circuits immune to dynamic hazards?

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — POS Hazard-Free Design

Given $F = (A+C)(\bar{A}+B)$. Set $C = 0$ and $B = 0$:
$$F = A \cdot \bar{A} = 0 \quad \text{(logically)}$$

**Cause of Static-0 Hazard:** When $A$ transitions $0 \to 1$, the OR-AND circuit experiences a race between the two maxterms.

**Consensus Maxterm** of $(A+C)$ and $(\bar{A}+B)$ with respect to $A$ is $(B+C)$.

**Hazard-Free POS:**
$$F_{HF} = (A+C)(\bar{A}+B)(B+C)$$

**Verification** at $B=0, C=0$:
$$F_{HF} = (A+0)(0+\bar{A})(0+0) = A \cdot \bar{A} \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \text{(Glitch-Free)}$$

#### Part (b) — Dynamic Hazards Explanation

Consider a 3-level circuit: $F = \overline{(\bar{A} \cdot B) + (A \cdot C)}$. For a transition of $A$ from $0 \to 1$ while $B = C = 1$:

- **Level 1**: $A$ and $\bar{A}$ race.
- **Level 2**: AND gates compute.
- **Level 3**: OR + NOT gate produces output.

Because the output is supposed to change from $0 \to 1$, but during the multi-level race it may exhibit $0 \to 1 \to 0 \to 1$ transitions before settling, this is a **dynamic hazard**.

**Why Two-Level Circuits Are Immune:** In a two-level SOP/POS network, a single input variable can propagate through only **one AND/OR level** before reaching the output OR/AND gate. There is no intermediate gate to create a *secondary* race condition. Dynamic hazards require at least **three logic levels** where the changing input can re-enter a gate that has already settled, creating a multi-stage race.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SOP and POS consensus forms** — Consensus is a *product* in SOP and a *sum* in POS. Mixing them is a 3-mark penalty.
> 2. **Omitting the K-map** — KTU examiners expect a fully drawn K-map with all prime implicants AND all consensus terms circled, not just the algebraic answer. **–2 marks** if missing.
> 3. **Not specifying the hazard type** — Always state "Static-1" or "Static-0" explicitly. Generic "static hazard" answers lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the consensus term** — Always substitute the critical input condition to *prove* the output remains constant.
> 5. **Adding extra redundant terms unnecessarily** — A consensus term is only valid if it bridges *logically adjacent* minterms. Random extra terms are incorrect and lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition**: Hazard = unwanted transient pulse due to gate delay mismatch.
- **Static-1 Hazard** ($S_1$): $1 \to 0 \to 1$ glitch in two-level **AND-OR (SOP)**.
- **Static-0 Hazard** ($S_0$): $0 \to 1 \to 0$ glitch in two-level **OR-AND (POS)**.
- **Dynamic Hazard**: Multi-transition glitch; occurs **only in 3+ level circuits**.
- **Root Cause**: Inverter delay creates a transient "gap" in coverage.
- **Cure for $S_1$**: Add **consensus product term** to SOP.
- **Cure for $S_0$**: Add **consensus sum term** to POS.
- **K-Map Rule**: Adjacent 1-cells (or 0-cells for POS) differing in **one variable** must be bridged by an extra implicant.
- **Existence Condition**: A static hazard exists only if **two adjacent minterms are covered by different prime implicants** in the minimal cover.
- **Test Inputs**: For $F = AB + \bar{A}C$, set the *non-transitioning* variables to the values that activate the consensus-vulnerable region ($B=C=1$ for SOP example).
- **Glitch Width**: Bounded by $2 \cdot t_{pd,inverter}$ in typical SOP.
- **Two-Level Theorem**: A *minimal* two-level SOP/POS is **never** hazard-free — consensus terms are mandatory.
- **Dynamic Hazard Theorem**: Two-level circuits are **provably immune** to dynamic hazards.
- **POS Adjacency Test**: Use maxterm pairs differing in 1 variable.
- **Engineering Impact**: Hazards corrupt synchronous flip-flop data and cause asynchronous race conditions.
- **Always**: Draw the K-map, label all prime implicants, add consensus terms, draw gate-level schematic, and verify with the test input set.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Synchronizers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT402 - DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Synchronizers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Data Synchronizer** is a sequential digital circuit (typically built using flip-flops) specifically engineered to safely transfer asynchronous data signals into a synchronous clock domain of a receiving system, while managing the unpredictable, random timing relationship between the independent clocks to prevent **metastability** propagation and system failure.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEVT402 - Digital System Design)**, data synchronizers are classified as essential **Clock Domain Crossing (CDC)** interface circuits, forming a mandatory bridge between two subsystems operating on physically or logically separate clock sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, students must demonstrate mastery in designing reliable multi-clock domain systems, identifying the role of synchronizers in Mixed-Signal SoC (System-on-Chip) and FPGA architectures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two railway platforms run by different railway companies with completely uncoordinated schedules (two different clocks). A passenger (the data bit) arrives at Platform A based on Company A's timing, but needs to safely board the train at Platform B (the receiving system), which runs on Company B's schedule.

- If the passenger simply leaps without checking, they might fall into the gap between the two trains — this is **METASTABILITY**.
- A **Data Synchronizer** acts like a **safety guard and waiting room**. The passenger is held in a safe holding area (a flip-flop) and only released when Company B's train is at a safe, fully-formed stop signal (a stable clock edge). If the timing is too tight (close to the edge), the guard waits for the *next* safe train (a second flip-flop), giving the passenger a full cycle to settle down.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Data Synchronizers?

In modern digital systems, it is common to have:
- **Multiple independent clocks** generated by separate PLLs or oscillators.
- **Peripheral interfaces** running at frequencies unrelated to the CPU clock.
- **Communication protocols** (UART, SPI, I2C) where external data is, by definition, **asynchronous** to the local clock.

When an asynchronous signal is sampled by a flip-flop, the setup ($t_{su}$) or hold ($t_h$) time may be violated, causing the flip-flop output to enter a **metastable state** — an unpredictable voltage level between logic 0 and logic 1 that may take an unbounded amount of time to resolve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in Industry:** Synchronizer reliability is universally quantified using the **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**, typically targeted to be in the order of **years to centuries** for safety-critical systems (avionics, medical, automotive ASIL-D).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Period | $T_{clk}$ | Time between two consecutive rising edges of the destination clock |
| Metastability Resolution Time | $t_r$ | Time required for a metastable node to settle to a valid logic level |
| Setup Time | $t_{su}$ | Minimum time data must be stable before the clock edge |
| Hold Time | $t_h$ | Minimum time data must remain stable after the clock edge |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | Average expected operational time before a metastability-induced system error occurs |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Metastability

A flip-flop is a **bistable** device with two stable equilibrium points (logic 0 and logic 1). However, when the input data transition occurs *exactly* at the sampling instant of the clock edge, the flip-flop is pushed into a **quasi-stable metastable state**.

The metastable state behaves as a damped exponential decay governed by the following expression:

$$P(t) = e^{-t / \tau}$$

Where:
- $P(t)$ is the probability that the flip-flop is *still* metastable after time $t$.
- $\tau$ is the **metastability time constant**, a technology-dependent parameter (typically **20 ps to 50 ps** for a 28 nm CMOS process).

### 2.1.1 Why It Cannot Be Eliminated
Metastability is a **fundamental physical phenomenon**, not a design flaw. No matter how well the circuit is designed, there is *always* a non-zero probability that any given asynchronous input transition will occur within the setup/hold window. The synchronizer does not *eliminate* metastability; it merely **reduces its probability of propagating to a level that is statistically negligible over the system's lifetime**.

## 2.2 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) — The Industry Metric

The standard industrial model for synchronizer reliability is given by the **MTBF equation**:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{t_{r} / \tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$$

Where:
- $t_{r}$ is the **allowable resolution time** available to the synchronizer (i.e., the time from the first clock edge to the second clock edge minus internal logic delay). For a single-flop synchronizer, $t_r \approx T_{clk} - t_{su}$.
- $\tau$ is the **technology-dependent metastability time constant**.
- $T_0$ is the **flip-flop output settling time constant** (also technology-dependent, often $\tau \le T_0 \le 2\tau$).
- $f_{clk}$ is the **frequency of the receiving (destination) clock domain**.
- $f_{data}$ is the **asynchronous event arrival rate** at the synchronizer input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the exponential term dominates:** Because of $e^{t_r / \tau}$, even a tiny increase in $t_r$ (e.g., adding one more flip-flop stage to the synchronizer chain) causes a **multi-order-of-magnitude increase in MTBF**. This is why cascading flip-flops is so effective.

## 2.3 The Two-Flop Synchronizer (Standard Solution)

The simplest and most widely deployed CDC circuit is the **Two-Flop Synchronizer**, also known as the **basic synchronizer chain**.

### 2.3.1 Structural Logic
- **Stage 1 (FF1):** Samples the asynchronous data input `D_async`. This is the flip-flop most likely to enter metastability.
- **Stage 2 (FF2):** Samples the output of FF1 one full destination clock cycle later. By this time, with overwhelming probability, FF1 has resolved to a stable logic level. The downstream logic now sees a clean, synchronous signal.

### 2.3.2 Analytical Resolution
For a 2-flop synchronizer, the available resolution time is:

$$t_{r,2\text{-flop}} \approx T_{clk} - t_{su,FF2}$$

Compared to a 1-flop synchronizer, the 2-flop version provides an extra $T_{clk}$ of settling time, which **exponentially** boosts MTBF.

## 2.4 The Three-Flop Synchronizer (Extended Chain)

When even higher MTBF is required (e.g., in aerospace or medical systems), a **third flip-flop** is cascaded. The available resolution time becomes:

$$t_{r,3\text{-flop}} \approx 2 \cdot T_{clk} - t_{su}$$

The benefit, however, is **diminishing** — adding a third flop typically increases MTBF by several orders of magnitude, but adding a fourth flop rarely justifies the additional latency cost. In practice, the industry standard is:
- **2 flops:** > 99% of all CDC paths in commercial ASICs/FPGAs.
- **3 flops:** Safety-critical / aerospace / MIL-spec designs.
- **4+ flops:** Almost never used (latency penalty outweighs MTBF gain).

## 2.5 Advanced Synchronizer Architectures

### 2.5.1 Handshake Synchronizer
Used when transferring **multi-bit data words** between clock domains. Uses two control signals (request `REQ` and acknowledge `ACK`) crossed independently with 2-flop synchronizers. Guarantees data integrity at the cost of throughput.

### 2.5.2 FIFO-Based Synchronizer (Async FIFO)
The **gold standard** for high-throughput multi-bit CDC. Uses a dual-port memory (RAM) and two pointers (write pointer in source domain, read pointer in destination domain). The pointers are converted between clock domains using **Gray code + 2-flop synchronizers**, ensuring that only **one bit changes at a time** to avoid multi-bit CDC errors.

### 2.5.3 Pulse Synchronizer
Used for transmitting single pulses (events) shorter than the destination clock period. The pulse is stretched (e.g., by an XOR feedback loop) into a level signal, then re-converted into a one-cycle pulse in the destination domain.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Application | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|
| $P(t) = e^{-t / \tau}$ | Probability that flip-flop is still metastable after time $t$ | $t, \tau$ |
| $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{e^{t_r / \tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$ | Reliability of a 1-flop synchronizer | $t_r, \tau, T_0, f_{clk}, f_{data}$ |
| $t_{r,2\text{-flop}} = T_{clk} - t_{su}$ | Available resolution time for a 2-flop synchronizer | $T_{clk}, t_{su}$ |
| $t_{r,3\text{-flop}} = 2 \cdot T_{clk} - t_{su}$ | Available resolution time for a 3-flop synchronizer | $T_{clk}, t_{su}$ |
| $\text{MTBF}_{\text{enhanced}} \approx \text{MTBF}_{\text{base}} \cdot e^{T_{clk} / \tau}$ | MTBF gain by adding an extra flop stage | $T_{clk}, \tau$ |
| Gray Code Constraint: $b_{i+1} = b_i \oplus b_{i-1}$ | Encoding pointers for safe multi-bit CDC | $b_i$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Data synchronizers are **non-negotiable** in:
- **FPGA designs** (Xilinx 7-series, Intel Cyclone, etc.) where multiple **MMCM/PLL** outputs create dozens of clock domains.
- **ASIC SoCs** (mobile processors, networking chips) integrating IP blocks from multiple vendors with independent clocks.
- **Memory interfaces** (DDR, QDR) where the memory bus clock and the controller clock are physically separate.
- **Industrial / Automotive** (ISO 26262, ASIL-D) where undetected metastability can lead to **life-threatening failures**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Did You Know?** Modern FPGA vendors (e.g., Xilinx) provide dedicated hardware primitives like `XPM_CDC_SINGLE` and `XPM_CDC_GRAY` that have pre-characterized metastability parameters ($\tau$, $T_0$), so designers don't need to compute MTBF manually for CDC paths inside the FPGA fabric.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the MTBF Equation

The derivation begins with the **probability density** of an asynchronous data transition occurring within the setup/hold window of a flip-flop.

### Step 1 — Probability of Sampling Within the Metastability Window

For an asynchronous data signal with frequency $f_{data}$ and a destination clock of frequency $f_{clk}$, the probability that an asynchronous edge falls within the critical window of duration $W$ (where $W = t_{su} + t_h$) around a clock edge is:

$$P_{\text{critical}} = f_{data} \cdot W$$

This is the **raw probability per clock cycle** that a metastable event can be triggered.

### Step 2 — Conditional Probability of Unresolved Metastability

Given that a metastable event was triggered, the probability that it has **not** yet resolved after time $t_r$ (the available settling time) is given by the exponential decay:

$$P_{\text{unresolved}}(t_r) = e^{-t_r / \tau}$$

### Step 3 — Combined Failure Probability Per Cycle

Multiplying Step 1 and Step 2, the probability of a metastability-induced failure per destination clock cycle is:

$$P_{\text{fail,cycle}} = f_{data} \cdot W \cdot e^{-t_r / \tau}$$

### Step 4 — Converting to Time Domain (MTBF)

Since failures occur at a rate of $P_{\text{fail,cycle}}$ per cycle, and the clock cycles at frequency $f_{clk}$, the failure rate per second is:

$$\lambda = f_{clk} \cdot P_{\text{fail,cycle}} = f_{clk} \cdot f_{data} \cdot W \cdot e^{-t_r / \tau}$$

The Mean Time Between Failures is the reciprocal of the failure rate:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{f_{clk} \cdot f_{data} \cdot W \cdot e^{-t_r / \tau}}$$

### Step 5 — Standard Industrial Reformulation

The industry standard folds $W$ into a technology-dependent constant $T_0$ (since $W$ and $\tau$ are correlated at the transistor level), producing the canonical form:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{t_r / \tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Problem: Compute MTBF for a 2-Flop Synchronizer

**Given Data:**
- Destination clock frequency: $f_{clk} = 100$ MHz
- Asynchronous data toggle rate: $f_{data} = 10$ MHz
- Flip-flop metastability time constant: $\tau = 50$ ps
- Technology constant: $T_0 = 30$ ps
- Setup time of FF2: $t_{su} = 100$ ps

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period.**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the available resolution time for a 2-flop synchronizer.**

$$t_{r} = T_{clk} - t_{su} = 10 \text{ ns} - 100 \text{ ps} = 9.9 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponential numerator.**

$$\frac{t_r}{\tau} = \frac{9.9 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-12}} = 198$$

$$e^{198} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{86}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data} = (30 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (100 \times 10^6) \cdot (10 \times 10^6) = 3 \times 10^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Compute MTBF.**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{86}}{3 \times 10^{3}} \approx 8.3 \times 10^{82} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to years for intuition.**

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{years}} = \frac{8.3 \times 10^{82}}{3.154 \times 10^7} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{75} \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The MTBF is roughly $2.6 \times 10^{75}$ years, which is astronomically larger than the age of the universe ($\approx 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years). This confirms that the 2-flop synchronizer is statistically safe for **all practical purposes** at these clock frequencies.

## 3.3 Analytical Problem: Compare 1-Flop vs 2-Flop vs 3-Flop MTBF

Using the same parameters, compute the MTBF for each configuration:

**Step 1 — 1-Flop synchronizer resolution time.**

$$t_{r,1} = 0 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(no margin available before output is sampled)}$$

In practice, $t_{r,1} \approx 0$, so:

$$\text{MTBF}_{1\text{-flop}} \approx \frac{e^{0}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^3} \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — 2-Flop synchronizer (recompute).**

$$t_{r,2} = T_{clk} - t_{su} = 9.9 \text{ ns}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{2\text{-flop}} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{75} \text{ years}$$

**Step 3 — 3-Flop synchronizer resolution time.**

$$t_{r,3} = 2 \cdot T_{clk} - t_{su} = 2(10 \text{ ns}) - 100 \text{ ps} = 19.9 \text{ ns}$$

$$\frac{t_{r,3}}{\tau} = \frac{19.9 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-12}} = 398$$

$$e^{398} \approx 10^{173}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{3\text{-flop}} \approx 10^{166} \text{ years}$$

**Step 4 — Comparative analysis.**

| Configuration | $t_r$ | MTBF | Years |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1-Flop | 0 ns | $3.3 \times 10^{-4}$ s | $\approx 0$ |
| 2-Flop | 9.9 ns | $8.3 \times 10^{82}$ s | $\approx 10^{75}$ years |
| 3-Flop | 19.9 ns | $\approx 10^{173}$ years | $\approx 10^{166}$ years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The 1-flop synchronizer is essentially **useless** — it fails every fraction of a millisecond. The 2-flop synchronizer provides an improvement of **75 orders of magnitude** at the cost of just 1 extra clock cycle of latency. This is why **2 flops is the universal default** in industry.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Two-Flop Data Synchronizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef async fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ff1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ff2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sync fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meta fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    D_ASYNC["D ASYNC INPUT SOURCE CLOCK DOMAIN"]:::async
    FF1["FF1 METASTABLE PRONE STAGE 1"]:::ff1
    FF2["FF2 SETTLED SYNCHRONOUS STAGE 2"]:::ff2
    OUT["D SYNC CLEAN OUTPUT TO DEST DOMAIN"]:::sync
    META["METASTABLE WINDOW T SU T H VIOLATION ZONE"]:::meta
    CLK["DESTINATION CLOCK F CLK 100 MHZ"]:::sync

    D_ASYNC -->|"Async edge arrives at random phase"| FF1
    FF1 -->|"Possibly metastable"| FF2
    FF2 -->|"Resolved stable logic"| OUT
    FF1 -.->|"Sampling hazard"| META
    CLK -->|"Clock line to both stages"| FF1
    CLK -->|"Same clock line delayed by 1 cycle"| FF2
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Multi-Bit CDC Error Mechanism (Why Naive Sync Fails)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef source fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    SRC["MULTI BIT DATA BUS BINARY ENCODED 4 BITS"]:::source
    FF_A["FLIP FLOP A SAMPLES BIT 0"]:::bad
    FF_B["FLIP FLOP B SAMPLES BIT 1"]:::bad
    FF_C["FLIP FLOP C SAMPLES BIT 2"]:::bad
    FF_D["FLIP FLOP D SAMPLES BIT 3"]:::bad
    ERR["CORRUPTED VALUE BITS ARRIVE AT DIFFERENT CYCLES"]:::bad
    GRAY["GRAY CODE POINTER 1 BIT CHANGES AT A TIME"]:::good
    SAFE["ALL BITS ARRIVE CONSISTENTLY NO SKEW ERROR"]:::good

    SRC --> FF_A
    SRC --> FF_B
    SRC --> FF_C
    SRC --> FF_D
    FF_A --> ERR
    FF_B --> ERR
    FF_C --> ERR
    FF_D --> ERR

    SRC --> GRAY
    GRAY --> SAFE
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Asynchronous FIFO Synchronizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef wr fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ram fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef rd fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sync fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    WR_DOMAIN["WRITE CLOCK DOMAIN F WCLK 200 MHZ"]:::wr
    WR_PTR["WRITE POINTER BINARY COUNTER 5 BITS"]:::wr
    GRAY_ENC["GRAY CODE ENCODER"]:::wr
    SYNC1["2 FLOP SYNCHRONIZER TO READ DOMAIN"]:::sync
    GRAY_DEC["GRAY CODE DECODER"]:::rd
    RD_PTR["READ POINTER BINARY COUNTER 5 BITS"]:::rd
    FIFO_MEM["DUAL PORT RAM MEMORY ARRAY"]:::ram
    FULL["FULL FLAG GENERATOR"]:::wr
    EMPTY["EMPTY FLAG GENERATOR"]:::rd
    RD_DOMAIN["READ CLOCK DOMAIN F RCLK 150 MHZ"]:::rd

    WR_DOMAIN --> WR_PTR
    WR_PTR --> GRAY_ENC
    GRAY_ENC -->|"gray coded write ptr"| SYNC1
    SYNC1 --> GRAY_DEC
    GRAY_DEC --> RD_PTR
    WR_PTR --> FIFO_MEM
    RD_PTR --> FIFO_MEM
    WR_PTR --> FULL
    RD_PTR --> EMPTY
    FIFO_MEM --> RD_DOMAIN
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Synchronizer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef start fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef q1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef q2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef q3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ans fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    START["DATA TO BE TRANSFERRED BETWEEN CLOCK DOMAINS"]:::start
    Q1["IS THE DATA A SINGLE BIT OR CONTROL SIGNAL"]:::q1
    Q2["ARE YOU TRANSFERRING A MULTI BIT DATA WORD"]:::q2
    Q3["DO YOU NEED HIGH THROUGHPUT BULK DATA TRANSFER"]:::q3
    A1["USE 2 FLOP SYNCHRONIZER BASIC CHAIN"]:::ans
    A2["USE HANDSHAKE SYNCHRONIZER REQ ACK"]:::ans
    A3["USE ASYNCHRONOUS FIFO WITH GRAY CODED POINTERS"]:::ans
    A4["USE PULSE SYNCHRONIZER XOR FEEDBACK STRETCHER"]:::ans

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|"YES SINGLE BIT"| A1
    Q1 -->|"NO MULTI BIT"| Q2
    Q2 -->|"YES"| Q3
    Q3 -->|"NO LOW THROUGHPUT OK"| A2
    Q3 -->|"YES HIGH THROUGHPUT"| A3
    Q2 -->|"PULSE OR EVENT TYPE"| A4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**What is metastability in digital systems, and why is it a concern in Clock Domain Crossing (CDC)?**

**Model Answer:**

Metastability is a transient anomalous state in which a bistable element (such as a flip-flop) settles at an unpredictable voltage level between valid logic 0 and logic 1, due to setup or hold time violations. It arises when asynchronous data transitions occur too close to the active edge of the destination clock.

It is a concern in CDC because:
- The metastable voltage may propagate to downstream combinational logic, causing **erroneous system behavior**.
- The time required to resolve to a valid logic level is **statistically unbounded** (exponentially decaying, but never guaranteed to finish in a finite time).
- In safety-critical systems, even a single unresolved metastable event can cause **catastrophic system failure**.

(3 marks: definition 1, causes 1, CDC impact 1)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**List and briefly explain any three types of data synchronizers used in multi-clock domain designs.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Two-Flop Synchronizer:** A cascade of two flip-flops clocked by the destination clock. The first FF may enter metastability, but the second FF samples the (almost certainly) resolved output one full cycle later. Industry default for single-bit CDC.

2. **Handshake Synchronizer:** Uses request (`REQ`) and acknowledge (`ACK`) control signals, each synchronized with a 2-flop chain. Used for **multi-bit data words** when throughput is not critical.

3. **Asynchronous FIFO Synchronizer:** Uses dual-port memory and Gray-coded pointers. Gray code ensures only one bit changes at a time, eliminating multi-bit skew. The **gold standard** for high-throughput bulk data transfer between independent clock domains.

(3 marks: 1 mark each)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Design and analyze a 2-flop data synchronizer for transferring a single asynchronous control signal into a 100 MHz clock domain. Given that the metastability time constant $\tau = 40$ ps, technology constant $T_0 = 25$ ps, and asynchronous event rate $f_{data} = 5$ MHz, compute the MTBF. Compare it with a 1-flop synchronizer and justify why 2 flops is the industry standard.**

**Part (a) — Design the 2-flop synchronizer and derive the MTBF expression. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
The 2-flop synchronizer consists of:
- **FF1:** D-input connected to asynchronous signal `D_async`; clocked by destination clock `clk_dst`.
- **FF2:** D-input connected to Q-output of FF1; clocked by the same `clk_dst`.
- The Q-output of FF2 is the synchronized output `D_sync` used by the destination logic.

**Step 2 — Available Resolution Time:**
For a 2-flop synchronizer, the resolution time is one full destination clock period minus the setup time of FF2:

$$t_r = T_{clk} - t_{su,FF2} \approx T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — MTBF Formula:**

$$\text{MTBF}_{2\text{-flop}} = \frac{e^{t_r / \tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values:**

$$\frac{t_r}{\tau} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{40 \times 10^{-12}} = 250$$

$$e^{250} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{108}$$

$$T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data} = (25 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (100 \times 10^6) \cdot (5 \times 10^6) = 1.25 \times 10^4$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{2\text{-flop}} = \frac{3.7 \times 10^{108}}{1.25 \times 10^4} \approx 2.96 \times 10^{104} \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to years:

$$\text{MTBF}_{2\text{-flop,years}} = \frac{2.96 \times 10^{104}}{3.154 \times 10^7} \approx 9.4 \times 10^{96} \text{ years}$$

**Part (b) — Compare with 1-flop synchronizer and justify the choice. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — 1-Flop Synchronizer MTBF:**
With only 1 flip-flop, the available resolution time is essentially zero (the output is sampled by downstream logic on the same clock edge, leaving no settling margin). Taking $t_{r,1} \approx 0$:

$$\text{MTBF}_{1\text{-flop}} = \frac{e^{0}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}} = \frac{1}{1.25 \times 10^4} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ seconds} \approx 80 \text{ microseconds}$$

**Step 2 — Quantitative Comparison:**

| Parameter | 1-Flop | 2-Flop |
|---|---|---|
| $t_r$ | 0 ns | 10 ns |
| $t_r / \tau$ | 0 | 250 |
| MTBF | $80 \, \mu s$ | $9.4 \times 10^{96}$ years |
| Ratio | 1 | $\approx 10^{108} \times$ improvement |

**Step 3 — Justification:**
- The 2-flop synchronizer provides **108 orders of magnitude** higher MTBF for the cost of just **1 extra clock cycle latency** (10 ns).
- In a 100 MHz system, a 10 ns latency is **typically negligible** in pipeline-critical paths.
- A 1-flop synchronizer fails every ~80 $\mu$s, making it **useless for any real application**.
- Therefore, 2 flops is the industry default: it offers virtually infinite reliability at an acceptable latency cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> Many students compute MTBF for 1-flop by reusing the 2-flop $t_r$ value, leading to a wildly wrong answer. **Remember:** for 1-flop, the resolution time is effectively **zero**, because the same clock edge that samples the asynchronous data also drives the downstream logic. Always set $t_{r,1} = 0$ explicitly and show the simplification $e^0 = 1$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Explain the limitations of using a 2-flop synchronizer for multi-bit data buses. Describe the asynchronous FIFO synchronizer architecture in detail, with a clear explanation of why Gray code is used for pointer synchronization. Compute the MTBF for the FIFO's pointer synchronizer if $f_{clk} = 200$ MHz, $f_{data} = 25$ MHz, $\tau = 30$ ps, and $T_0 = 20$ ps.**

**Part (a) — Limitations of 2-flop synchronizer and FIFO architecture explanation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Limitations of 2-Flop for Multi-Bit Data:**
If a multi-bit bus is passed through independent 2-flop chains, each bit experiences **slightly different metastability resolution delays**. The downstream logic may then sample a *mixed* combination of old and new bits, producing a **corrupted value** (e.g., transitioning from `0011` to `0100`, but the synchronizer temporarily reads `0000` or `0111`). This is called a **multi-bit CDC skew error**.

**Step 2 — Asynchronous FIFO Architecture:**
The async FIFO consists of:
1. **Dual-Port RAM:** One write port, one read port, with independent clocks.
2. **Write Pointer (Binary Counter):** Increments in the source (write) clock domain, points to the next write address.
3. **Read Pointer (Binary Counter):** Increments in the destination (read) clock domain, points to the next read address.
4. **Gray-Coded Pointers:** Each binary pointer is converted to Gray code **before** crossing the clock domain boundary.
5. **2-Flop Synchronizers:** The Gray-coded pointer from one domain is synchronized into the other domain using a 2-flop chain.
6. **Full/Empty Flags:** Generated by comparing the synchronized pointer (in the local domain) with the local pointer.

**Step 3 — Why Gray Code is Mandatory for Multi-Bit CDC:**
Gray code has the property that **only ONE bit changes** between consecutive integer values. For example, transitioning from 3 (`011`) to 4 (`100`):

- Binary: `011` → `100` (all 3 bits change simultaneously).
- Gray:    `010` → `110` (only 1 bit changes).

Since the 2-flop synchronizer guarantees that the *output* is consistent (single bit transition), even if the input is metastable, only that one bit can be wrong — and since the destination reads the pointer **after** the synchronizer, it is guaranteed to read a value that is either the *old* pointer or the *new* pointer (both of which are valid FIFO states). This eliminates multi-bit skew errors entirely.

**Part (b) — Compute the MTBF for the FIFO's pointer synchronizer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Available Resolution Time for the 2-Flop Pointer Synchronizer:**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^6} = 5 \text{ ns}$$

$$t_r \approx T_{clk} = 5 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the MTBF Formula:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{t_r / \tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$\frac{t_r}{\tau} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{30 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 166.67$$

$$e^{166.67} \approx 1.3 \times 10^{72}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Denominator:**

$$T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data} = (20 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (200 \times 10^6) \cdot (25 \times 10^6) = 1 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 5 — Final MTBF:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^{72}}{1 \times 10^{5}} = 1.3 \times 10^{67} \text{ seconds}$$

In years:

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{years}} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^{67}}{3.154 \times 10^7} \approx 4.1 \times 10^{59} \text{ years}$$

**Conclusion:** Even at the higher frequencies specified, the 2-flop Gray-coded pointer synchronizer provides an MTBF vastly exceeding the age of the universe — fully validating the FIFO architecture for high-throughput multi-bit CDC.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part a):**
> A common mistake is to explain FIFO as a "buffer with two clocks" without specifying the **role of Gray code** in the pointer synchronizers. Marks for this question are heavily weighted on the Gray code justification (at least 3 out of 7 marks). If you omit the binary-to-Gray conversion, you will lose significant marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Metastability is a fundamental physical phenomenon** — it cannot be eliminated, only managed to a statistically negligible probability.
- [x] The **MTBF equation** is the industry-standard metric for synchronizer reliability: $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{e^{t_r / \tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$.
