<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between behavioral and structural modeling styles in VHDL with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Behavioral | Structural |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| **Abstraction** | High (algorithm) | Low (gate-level) |
| **Constructs used** | `process`, sequential statements | `component`, `port map` |
| **Execution** | Sequential inside process | Concurrent instantiation |
| **Example — MUX 2:1** | `process(a,b,s) begin if s='1' then y<=a; else y<=b; end if; end process;` | `component and2 ... U1: and2 port map(...); U2: and2 port map(...);` |
| **Synthesis result** | May or may not infer hardware directly | Always maps to a netlist |

> **[1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for synthesis implication.]**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the sensitivity list in a VHDL process? What happens if a signal read inside the process is omitted from the sensitivity list?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The **sensitivity list** is the set of signals that, when they change value, **re-activate** the process. (1 mark)

2. If a signal is read inside the process but **omitted** from the sensitivity list, the process will **not re-execute** when that signal changes. This leads to: (1 mark)
   - **Latch inference** (in synthesis) — unwanted memory elements.
   - **Simulation mismatch** between pre- and post-synthesis models.

3. **Rule of thumb:** For combinational processes, every input signal read must appear in the sensitivity list. (1 mark)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Choice 1]`

**(a) Explain the three modeling styles in VHDL with neat VHDL code fragments for a 2:1 multiplexer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Entity (common to all three):**

```vhdl
entity mux2_1 is
    port (a, b, sel : in  bit;
          y         : out bit);
end mux2_1;
```

**1. Behavioral Model (using if-then-else inside a process):**

```vhdl
architecture behavioral of mux2_1 is
begin
    process (a, b, sel)
    begin
        if sel = '1' then
            y <= a;
        else
            y <= b;
        end if;
    end process;
end behavioral;
```
**[2 marks for code + 1 mark for explanation]**

**2. Dataflow Model (using concurrent `when-else`):**

```vhdl
architecture dataflow of mux2_1 is
begin
    y <= a when sel = '1' else b;
end dataflow;
```
**[1 mark for code + 1 mark for explanation]**

**3. Structural Model (using AND/OR gates with inverter):**

```vhdl
architecture structural of mux2_1 is
    component and2 port (x, y : in bit; z : out bit); end component;
    component or2  port (x, y : in bit; z : out bit); end component;
    component inv  port (x    : in bit; y : out bit); end component;
    signal nsel, t1, t2 : bit;
begin
    G1 : inv  port map (sel, nsel);
    G2 : and2 port map (a, sel, t1);
    G3 : and2 port map (b, nsel, t2);
    G4 : or2  port map (t1, t2, y);
end structural;
```
**[1 mark for component declaration + 1 mark for instantiations/port map]**

**Boolean equivalence of structural model:**

$$y = (a \cdot \text{sel}) + (b \cdot \overline{\text{sel}})$$

This is the standard 2:1 MUX equation — synthesizes to the same hardware. **[0.5 marks for equation]**

---

**(b) Design a 4-bit ripple carry adder using structural VHDL style with a 1-bit full adder as a component. Draw the block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — 1-bit Full Adder Component (reused):**

```vhdl
entity full_adder is
    port (A, B, Cin : in  bit;
          S, Cout   : out bit);
end full_adder;

architecture fa_beh of full_adder is
begin
    process (A, B, Cin)
    begin
        if A = '0' and B = '0' and Cin = '0' then S <= '0';
        elsif A = '0' and B = '0' and Cin = '1' then S <= '1';
        elsif A = '0' and B = '1' and Cin = '0' then S <= '1';
        elsif A = '0' and B = '1' and Cin = '1' then S <= '0';
        elsif A = '1' and B = '0' and Cin = '0' then S <= '1';
        elsif A = '1' and B = '0' and Cin = '1' then S <= '0';
        elsif A = '1' and B = '1' and Cin = '0' then S <= '0';
        else S <= '1';
        end if;
    end process;
end fa_beh;
```

**Step 2 — 4-bit RCA Top Entity:**

```vhdl
entity rca_4bit is
    port (A, B : in  bit_vector(3 downto 0);
          Cin  : in  bit;
          S    : out bit_vector(3 downto 0);
          Cout : out bit);
end rca_4bit;
```

**Step 3 — Structural Architecture with Generate:**

```vhdl
architecture structural of rca_4bit is
    component full_adder
        port (A, B, Cin : in  bit;
              S, Cout   : out bit);
    end component;
    signal C : bit_vector(4 downto 0);
begin
    C(0) <= Cin;

    AD : for i in 0 to 3 generate
        FA : full_adder port map (A(i), B(i), C(i), S(i), C(i+1));
    end generate AD;

    Cout <= C(4);
end structural;
```

**[3 marks for code: 1 mark entity, 1 mark component, 1 mark generate + port map]**

**Step 4 — Block Diagram Description:**

```
   A(3) B(3) C(3)         A(0) B(0) C(0)
     \  |   /               \  |   /
      \ | /                   \ | /
     [FA3] --C(4)=Cout      [FA0] --C(1)
        |                      |
       S(3)                   S(0)
       ...      C(3) ... C(1)  ...     Cin
```

Total components: **4 full adders.** Gate count: $4 \times 5 = 20$ gates. Propagation delay: $4 \times t_{\text{pd,FA}}$. **[1 mark for block diagram + 1 mark for analysis]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Missing `end generate` label** — causes syntax error. (-0.5 mark)
> 2. **Wrong carry index** — students often wire `C(i)` to the wrong port. The carry-out of stage $i$ must connect to the carry-in of stage $i+1$. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting `C(0) <= Cin`** — the first full adder has no input carry otherwise. (-1 mark)
> 4. **No `bit_vector` declaration** for `A, B, S` — results in type mismatch. (-0.5 mark)

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice 2]`

**(a) Explain the VHDL process statement with its syntax. Differentiate between signal and variable with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Process Statement — Syntax:**

```vhdl
[label :] process (sensitivity_list)
    [declarations of constants, variables, types]
begin
    sequential_statements;
end process [label];
```

**Key Characteristics (3 marks):**

1. A process is a **concurrent statement**; multiple processes run **in parallel** at simulation time.
2. Inside the process, statements execute **sequentially** (top to bottom).
3. The process **executes once** at time 0, then **suspends** until any signal in the sensitivity list changes.
4. All signal assignments inside a process use `<=` and **take effect only at the end of the process** (after the final `end process`).
5. A process with **no sensitivity list** must contain at least one `wait` statement.

**Signal vs Variable — Tabular Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Signal | Variable |
|---------|--------|----------|
| Keyword | `<=` (assignment) | `:=` (assignment) |
| Declaration | `signal s : bit;` | `variable v : bit;` |
| Update timing | At end of process (with delta delay) | Immediately (no delay) |
| Scope | Visible across the entire architecture | Local to the process/function |
| Hardware mapping | Wires and flip-flops | Not synthesized to hardware |
| Default value | None (unless specified) | Initial value optional at declaration |

**Example Code Demonstrating the Difference (1 mark):**

```vhdl
process (a, b)
    variable temp : bit := '0';
begin
    temp := a;            -- Variable: updated immediately
    temp := temp xor b;   -- Uses NEW value of temp
    y_sig <= temp;        -- Signal y_sig gets the final temp
end process;
```

If `temp` were a `signal`, the second line would read the **old** value of `temp` (since signal updates are deferred), giving the **wrong** result.

---

**(b) Write a behavioral VHDL model for a 4-to-1 multiplexer using a `case` statement. Verify the design with the truth table. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Entity Declaration:**

```vhdl
entity mux4_1 is
    port (d : in  bit_vector(3 downto 0);
          s : in  bit_vector(1 downto 0);
          y : out bit);
end mux4_1;
```

**Step 2 — Behavioral Architecture:**

```vhdl
architecture behavioral of mux4_1 is
begin
    process (d, s)
    begin
        case s is
            when "00"   => y <= d(0);
            when "01"   => y <= d(1);
            when "10"   => y <= d(2);
            when "11"   => y <= d(3);
            when others => y <= '0';
        end case;
    end process;
end behavioral;
```

**[2 marks for entity, 3 marks for process + case statement, 1 mark for `when others` clause]**

**Step 3 — Truth Table Verification (1 mark):**

| s(1) | s(0) | y | Boolean Expression |
|------|------|---|--------------------|
| 0 | 0 | d(0) | $y = \overline{s_1} \cdot \overline{s_0} \cdot d_0$ |
| 0 | 1 | d(1) | $y = \overline{s_1} \cdot s_0 \cdot d_1$ |
| 1 | 0 | d(2) | $y = s_1 \cdot \overline{s_0} \cdot d_2$ |
| 1 | 1 | d(3) | $y = s_1 \cdot s_0 \cdot d_3$ |

**Step 4 — General Boolean Expression (1 mark):**

$$y = \sum_{i=0}^{3} d_i \cdot m_i(s_1, s_0)$$

where $m_i$ is the $i$-th minterm of the select lines.

This 4:1 MUX is functionally equivalent to a 2:1 MUX built from 2:1 MUXes via the recursive relation:

$$\text{MUX4:1}(d_0, d_1, d_2, d_3; s_1, s_0) = \text{MUX2:1}\big(\text{MUX2:1}(d_0, d_1; s_0),\, \text{MUX2:1}(d_2, d_3; s_0);\, s_1\big)$$

This shows the structural decomposition of the behavioral model. **[0.5 marks bonus for this insight]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Incomplete case statement** — synthesis infers a **latch** if not all cases are covered. Always add `when others`. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Missing `d` in sensitivity list** — leads to stale outputs when data lines change. (-0.5 mark)
> 3. **Wrong bit ordering** — confusing `s(1) s(0)` indexing with binary value "10" vs "01". (-0.5 mark)
> 4. **Using `:=` instead of `<=` for output** — outputs are signals, not variables. (-0.5 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Behavioral & Structural Modeling**

- ✅ **Three modeling styles in VHDL:** Behavioral (process), Dataflow (concurrent assignment), Structural (component + port map).
- ✅ **Abstraction order:** Behavioral > Dataflow > Structural (highest to lowest).
- ✅ **Process statement** runs concurrently with other concurrent statements but executes **sequentially internally**.
- ✅ **Sensitivity list** must include **every signal read** in a combinational process — otherwise latches are inferred.
- ✅ **Signal vs Variable:** Signal uses `<=`, updates at end of process. Variable uses `:=`, updates immediately. Variables are local; signals are global.
- ✅ **Component Declaration** goes **inside architecture, before `begin`**. It is a *template*, not a call.
- ✅ **Port Map** can be **positional** (order matters) or **named** (order does not matter). Named is preferred for readability.
- ✅ **Generate statement** replicates a structure $N$ times — used for N-bit adders, register banks, etc.
- ✅ **Structural design** maps directly to a gate-level netlist, ideal for synthesis with predictable timing.
- ✅ **Behavioral design** is concise and good for FSMs, decoders, multiplexers.
- ✅ **Mixed-style modeling** is the industrial norm: control logic behaviorally, data path structurally.
- ✅ **Case statement** synthesizes to a multiplexer. Always include `when others` to avoid inferred latches.
- ✅ **Gate count of 1-bit full adder:** 5 gates (2 XOR, 2 AND, 1 OR).
- ✅ **Total gates in N-bit ripple carry adder:** $G_{\text{total}} = 5N$.
- ✅ **Critical path delay of N-bit RCA:** $t_{\text{pd,total}} = N \times t_{\text{pd,FA}}$.
- ✅ **7-segment display** has 7 segments (a–g) — common BCD-to-7-seg decoder exam favorite.
- ✅ **Always declare `bit_vector(N downto 0)`** for N+1 bit wide buses; `to` is ascending, `downto` is descending.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT402 - DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Synthesis ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** > *Synthesis* is the automated design step in the **ASIC (Application Specific Integrated Circuit) / Digital VLSI design flow** that converts a high-level **Register Transfer Level (RTL)** hardware description — written in an **HDL (Hardware Description Language)** such as **Verilog** or **VHDL** — into an optimized, technology-specific **Gate-Level Netlist** composed of standard cells from a target **Standard Cell Library**, while satisfying user-defined **design constraints** for *area*, *timing*, and *power*. In simpler board-exam language, *Synthesis = RTL → Gate-Level Netlist*. It is the bridge between the "logical" world (what the circuit does) and the "physical" world (how it will actually be built in silicon). ### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Translator" Chef Imagine you write a **recipe book** in English (your RTL Verilog code) describing how to cook a dish. A skilled **chef** (the synthesis tool, e.g., *Synopsys Design Compiler*, *Cadence Genus*, *Yosys*) reads the recipe and: 1. Understands the *intent* of the dish (functional correctness). 2. Looks at what **ingredients** are available in the kitchen (your **target technology library** — e.g., 28nm standard cells from TSMC). 3. Reorganizes and optimizes the recipe to make it **tastier (faster timing)**, **cheaper (smaller area)**, and **less wasteful (lower power)**. 4. Finally, hands you a **step-by-step cooking plan** (the **gate-level netlist**) listing exactly which standard cells to use and how to wire them. The chef cannot invent new ingredients — he can only use what is in the kitchen (the technology library). This is why synthesis is *technology-dependent*, while the RTL is *technology-independent*. ### 📌 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **RTL** | Register Transfer Level — behavioral/structural HDL description | | **Netlist** | A textual/graphical list of interconnected logic gates | | **Standard Cell Library** | Pre-characterized logic gates (NAND, NOR, DFF, etc.) for a specific fab process | | **GTECH** | Generic Technology — technology-independent primitive gates used internally by synthesis tools | | **SDC** | Synopsys Design Constraints — timing/area/power constraint file | | **Slack** | Timing margin — positive means timing is met, negative means violation | | **Critical Path** | The longest delay path determining the maximum operating frequency | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"What is Synthesis?"*, always include the phrase **"translates RTL to an optimized, technology-mapped gate-level netlist"** — this single sentence carries 1–2 marks by itself. ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Synthesis Optimization Synthesis tools fundamentally optimize across **three competing dimensions** — often called the *PPA Triad*: 1. **P**erformance (Timing / Speed) — measured in *ns* or *MHz*. 2. **P**ower — measured in *µW* or *mW* (Dynamic + Static). 3. **A**rea — measured in *gate count* or *µm²*. > [!IMPORTANT] > A synthesis tool is essentially a **multi-objective optimizer** running algorithms (such as *Quasi-Newton*, *Genetic Algorithms*, or *Simulated Annealing*) on the internal graph representation. Improving one metric almost always degrades another — this is the famous **PPA trade-off**. ### 2.2 The Synthesis Design Flow — Step-by-Step Logic A typical commercial synthesis flow consists of the following well-defined stages: | Step | Stage Name | What Happens | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **1** | **HDL Parsing / Elaboration** | Tool reads the Verilog/VHDL, checks syntax, builds an internal **Hierarchical Data Structure** (often a **DAG — Directed Acyclic Graph**). | | **2** | **Translation to GTECH** | HDL operators (`+`, `*`, `if-else`, `case`) are converted to **technology-independent** generic logic primitives. | | **3** | **Constraint Reading (SDC)** | The `.sdc` file is parsed — clock period, input/output delays, false paths, multicycle paths, max fanout, max capacitance. | | **4** | **Architectural / High-Level Optimization** | Resource sharing, constant propagation, dead-code elimination, common sub-expression elimination, FSM encoding (binary, gray, one-hot). | | **5** | **Logic-Level Optimization** | Boolean minimization (K-map/Quine-McCluskey), don't-care optimization, two-level and multi-level logic optimization. | | **6** | **Technology Mapping** | GTECH primitives are mapped to actual cells from the **target standard cell library** (e.g., NAND2_X1, DFF_X1). | | **7** | **Incremental / Post-Mapping Optimization** | Buffer insertion, gate sizing, remapping, critical path refinement, fixing DRC violations (max transition, max capacitance). | | **8** | **Report Generation** | Area report, timing report, power report, QoR (Quality of Results) summary. | | **9** | **Netlist Export** | Final **gate-level Verilog/VHDL netlist** + **SDC** + **SDF (Standard Delay Format)** are written out for downstream P&R. | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > These are the **core timing equations** that appear repeatedly in KTU board exam questions on synthesis and timing analysis. | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Units | | :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Data Arrival Time** | $T_{arrival} = T_{clk\text{-}q} + \sum T_{combinational}$ | ns | | 2 | **Data Required Time** | $T_{required} = T_{clock} - T_{setup} - T_{uncertainty}$ | ns | | 3 | **Setup Slack** | $Slack_{setup} = T_{required} - T_{arrival}$ | ns | | 4 | **Hold Slack** | $Slack_{hold} = T_{arrival} - T_{hold}$ | ns | | 5 | **Maximum Operating Frequency** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk\text{-}q} + T_{combo\_max} + T_{setup}}$ | MHz | | 6 | **Dynamic Power (CMOS)** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | W | | 7 | **Static (Leakage) Power** | $P_{static} = I_{leakage} \cdot V_{DD}$ | W | | 8 | **Total Power** | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static}$ | W | | 9 | **Timing Constraint (Clock Period)** | $T_{clk} \geq T_{clk\text{-}q} + T_{combo} + T_{setup}$ | ns | | 10 | **Slack Sign Rule** | $Slack \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ **Timing Met**
$Slack < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Timing Violated** | — | > [!WARNING] > **Critical LaTeX Pitfall:** When writing slack equations on the exam, students often write `|Slack|` using vertical bars. **Never** use bare `\|` inside markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent formatting collapse. For example, write $\vert Slack \vert$ instead of `|Slack|`. ### 2.4 Why Synthesis Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility - **Time-to-Market:** A chip designed at RTL can be re-targeted to *any* new fab process (28nm → 7nm) by re-running synthesis with a new library — **massive engineering reuse**. - **Verification Bridge:** Synthesis generates a gate-level netlist that can be re-simulated with **gate-level timing (SDF back-annotation)** to catch real silicon bugs before tape-out. - **Design Space Exploration:** Engineers run synthesis with different optimization directives (*area_high_effort*, *timing_high_effort*, *power_high_effort*) to pick the best PPA point. - **Foundation for STA:** The synthesized netlist + SDC is the input to **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** tools like *Synopsys PrimeTime* and *Cadence Tempus*. ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Setup Time Slack Equation **Problem Setup:** A flip-flop `FF1` drives combinational logic which feeds `FF2`. The clock period is $T_{clk}$. **Step 1 — Identify the data path.** Data leaves `FF1` at the active clock edge, propagates through combinational gates, and must arrive at `FF2` *before* the next active clock edge minus the setup time. **Step 2 — Write the data arrival time at `FF2`'s D pin.** $$ T_{arrival} = T_{clk\text{-}q} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{g_i} $$ This is because the clock-to-Q delay of `FF1` ($T_{clk\text{-}q}$) plus the cumulative delay of all $n$ combinational gates in the path gives the total propagation time. **Step 3 — Write the data required time at `FF2`'s D pin.** The next clock edge occurs at $T_{clk}$. The data must be stable *setup time* $T_{setup}$ before this edge. So: $$ T_{required} = T_{clk} - T_{setup} $$ Adding clock jitter / uncertainty $T_{unc}$: $$ T_{required} = T_{clk} - T_{setup} - T_{unc} $$ **Step 4 — Define Setup Slack as the safety margin.** $$ Slack_{setup} = T_{required} - T_{arrival} $$ Substituting both expressions: $$ \begin{aligned} Slack_{setup} &= \bigl(T_{clk} - T_{setup} - T_{unc}\bigr) - \bigl(T_{clk\text{-}q} + \sum T_{g_i}\bigr) \\[6pt] &= T_{clk} \;-\; T_{setup} \;-\; T_{unc} \;-\; T_{clk\text{-}q} \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{g_i} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result.** - If $Slack_{setup} \geq 0$ → **Setup timing is satisfied**, the design meets frequency. - If $Slack_{setup} < 0$ → **Setup violation**, frequency must be reduced OR logic must be re-optimized. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 2-Mark Sub-Part) > **Given:** $T_{clk} = 10\ \text{ns},\ \ T_{clk\text{-}q} = 0.3\ \text{ns},\ \ T_{setup} = 0.2\ \text{ns},\ \ T_{unc} = 0.1\ \text{ns}$. > Combinational path contains 4 logic gates with delays: $0.4,\ 0.6,\ 0.5,\ 0.3\ \text{ns}$. **Step 1 — Sum the combinational delay.** $$ \sum T_{g_i} = 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 1.8\ \text{ns} $$ **Step 2 — Compute arrival time.** $$ T_{arrival} = 0.3 + 1.8 = 2.1\ \text{ns} $$ **Step 3 — Compute required time.** $$ T_{required} = 10 - 0.2 - 0.1 = 9.7\ \text{ns} $$ **Step 4 — Compute setup slack.** $$ \begin{aligned} Slack_{setup} &= T_{required} - T_{arrival} \\ &= 9.7 - 2.1 = 7.6\ \text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verify.** Since $7.6 \geq 0$, **setup timing is met with a 7.6 ns margin**. The design is comfortably running on a 100 MHz clock and could likely be pushed to a higher frequency. ### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Operating Frequency The maximum frequency is reached when $Slack_{setup} = 0$. Rearranging the slack equation: $$ T_{clk\_min} = T_{clk\text{-}q} + \sum T_{g_i} + T_{setup} + T_{unc} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} f_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk\_min}} \\ &= \frac{1}{T_{clk\text{-}q} + \sum T_{g_i} + T_{setup} + T_{unc}} \end{aligned} $$ For the worked example above: $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{2.1 + 0.2 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{2.4} \approx 416.67\ \text{MHz} $$ ### 3.4 Hold Time Slack Derivation Hold time is checked on the *same* clock edge — the new data from `FF1` must not propagate to `FF2`'s D pin before the hold time expires after the edge. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{arrival} &= T_{clk\text{-}q} + \sum T_{g_i} \\ T_{required} &= T_{hold} \\ Slack_{hold} &= T_{arrival} - T_{required} \end{aligned} $$ Hold slack is **independent of clock period** — it cannot be fixed by reducing frequency. Violations require inserting *delay buffers* in the data path. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Synthesis Optimization Goals | Optimization Goal | Command / Directive (Typical) | Effect on Area | Effect on Speed | Effect on Power | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Area minimization** | `set_max_area 0` | ↓ Decreases | ↑ May slow down | ↓ Decreases (smaller caps) | | **Timing / Speed** | `set_max_delay` / `-timing_high_effort` | ↑ May increase | ↓ Faster | ↑ May increase (sizing) | | **Power optimization** | `-power_effort high` | ~ Neutral | ~ Neutral | ↓ Decreases significantly | | **Balanced (default)** | `compile` | — | — | — | | **Boundary optimization** | `set_boundary_optimization true` | ✓ Better for I/O | ✓ Better I/O timing | ✓ Better I/O power | ## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 High-Level Synthesis Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["RTL HDL Source Verilog or VHDL"] --> B["HDL Parsing and Elaboration"] B --> C["Translation to GTECH Generic Logic"] C --> D["SDC Constraint Application"] D --> E["Architectural Optimization"] E --> F["Logic Level Boolean Minimization"] F --> G["Technology Mapping to Standard Cell Library"] G --> H["Incremental Post Mapping Optimization"] H --> I["Design Rule Check DRC Fixing"] I --> J["Report Generation Area Timing Power"] J --> K["Gate Level Netlist Output Verilog"] K --> L["Downstream Place and Route and STA"] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000 style E fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style F fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style G fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000 style H fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000 style I fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000 style J fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style K fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style L fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: Synthesis Tool Internal Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUTS["Design Inputs"] R["RTL HDL"] L["Standard Cell Library .lib"] S["SDC Constraints"] end subgraph ENGINE["Synthesis Engine Core"] P["Parser and Elaborator"] T["Translator to GTECH"] O1["Architectural Optimizer"] O2["Boolean Logic Optimizer"] M["Technology Mapper"] O3["Incremental Optimizer"] end subgraph OUTPUTS["Synthesis Outputs"] N["Gate Level Netlist"] REP["Reports Area Timing Power"] SDF["SDF Delay File"] end R --> P L --> M S --> P P --> T T --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> M M --> O3 O3 --> N O3 --> REP O3 --> SDF style INPUTS fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 style ENGINE fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,color:#000 style OUTPUTS fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Timing Path Visualization for Slack Derivation ```mermaid flowchart LR FF1["FF1 Launch Flip Flop"] -->|Tclkq| G1["Combinational Gate 1"] G1 --> G2["Combinational Gate 2"] G2 --> G3["Combinational Gate 3"] G3 --> G4["Combinational Gate 4"] G4 -->|Arrival| FF2["FF2 Capture Flip Flop"] CLK["Clock Period Tclk"] -.->|Required minus Tsetup| FF2 style FF1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style FF2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style G1 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 style G2 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 style G3 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 style G4 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 style CLK fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagrams use the **block-level functional architecture** style mandated for non-physical rendering. The node IDs (`FF1`, `G1`, `CLK`, etc.) are purely alphanumeric, and the labels contain no markdown bolding or HTML — preserving perfect Mermaid compilation fidelity. ## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Define the term *Synthesis* in VLSI design. Differentiate between *RTL* and *Gate-Level Netlist*.** **Model Answer:** *Synthesis* is the automated process of converting a high-level hardware description written in an HDL (Verilog/VHDL) at the **Register Transfer Level (RTL)** into a **technology-mapped, optimized gate-level netlist** using cells from a target **Standard Cell Library**, while satisfying timing, area, and power constraints specified in an **SDC (Synopsys Design Constraints)** file. **Differentiation Table:** | Aspect | RTL | Gate-Level Netlist | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Abstraction | Behavioral / high-level | Structural / low-level | | Operators | Uses `+`, `*`, `if-else`, `case` | Uses `AND`, `OR`, `DFF`, `BUF` cells | | Technology | Independent | Dependent on target library | | Simulation Speed | Fast (no gate delays) | Slower (gate-level timing) | | Use | Design entry | Back-end P&R / STA input | --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **List any three optimization goals of a synthesis tool. What is the PPA trade-off?** **Model Answer:** The three primary optimization goals of a synthesis tool are: 1. **Area Optimization** — minimizing the number of gates / total silicon area used. 2. **Timing (Performance) Optimization** — minimizing critical path delay to meet clock frequency. 3. **Power Optimization** — minimizing dynamic and static power consumption. **PPA Trade-off:** PPA stands for **Power, Performance, and Area**. These three metrics are *mutually conflicting* — improving one usually degrades the other. For example, making a design faster (better performance) typically requires upsizing gates, which increases area and dynamic power. A synthesis tool's role is to find the *best compromise* based on user directives. --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice --- ## 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **complete synthesis design flow** with a neat block diagram. List the inputs and outputs of each major stage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A combinational path has the following gate delays: $T_{g1} = 0.5\ \text{ns},\ T_{g2} = 0.7\ \text{ns},\ T_{g3} = 0.4\ \text{ns}$. The flip-flops have $T_{clk\text{-}q} = 0.3\ \text{ns}$ and $T_{setup} = 0.2\ \text{ns}$. The clock period is $10\ \text{ns}$. Calculate the **setup slack** and the **maximum operating frequency**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Synthesis Design Flow:** *(See SECTION 4.1 for the block diagram. The textual flow is:)* 1. **HDL Parsing/Elaboration:** Tool reads the Verilog/VHDL source, performs syntax/semantic checks, and builds an internal hierarchical database. - *Inputs:* Verilog/VHDL source files. - *Outputs:* Internal database (`.ddc` / `.nlib`). 2. **Translation to GTECH:** HDL operators are mapped to technology-independent primitives like `*` → `MUL`, `+` → `ADD`, `if-else` → `MUX`. 3. **SDC Constraint Application:** The `.sdc` file (clock, IO delays, false paths) is loaded to guide optimization. 4. **Architectural Optimization:** High-level transforms — resource sharing, constant propagation, FSM encoding, dead-code elimination. 5. **Logic-Level Optimization:** Boolean minimization (Karnaugh map / Quine-McCluskey), two-level & multi-level optimization, don't-care optimization. 6. **Technology Mapping:** GTECH primitives replaced with actual cells from `.lib` (e.g., `NAND2_X1`, `DFF_X1`). 7. **Post-Mapping Optimization:** Buffer insertion, gate sizing, fix max transition/capacitance/fanout violations. 8. **Report Generation & Netlist Export:** Area, timing, power reports; gate-level Verilog netlist + SDF + updated SDC are written. *Final Output:* A technology-mapped **gate-level netlist** ready for **Place & Route**. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute the total combinational delay. $$ \sum T_{g_i} = 0.5 + 0.7 + 0.4 = 1.6\ \text{ns} $$ **Step 2:** Compute data arrival time at `FF2`. $$ T_{arrival} = T_{clk\text{-}q} + \sum T_{g_i} = 0.3 + 1.6 = 1.9\ \text{ns} $$ **Step 3:** Compute data required time at `FF2` (ignoring uncertainty for simplicity). $$ T_{required} = T_{clk} - T_{setup} = 10 - 0.2 = 9.8\ \text{ns} $$ **Step 4:** Compute setup slack. $$ \begin{aligned} Slack_{setup} &= T_{required} - T_{arrival} \\ &= 9.8 - 1.9 = 7.9\ \text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ Since $Slack_{setup} = 7.9\ \text{ns} \geq 0$, **setup timing is comfortably met**. **Step 5:** Compute maximum operating frequency. $$ \begin{aligned} f_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk\text{-}q} + \sum T_{g_i} + T_{setup}} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.3 + 1.6 + 0.2} = \frac{1}{2.1} \approx 476.19\ \text{MHz} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $Slack_{setup} = 7.9\ \text{ns}$ and $f_{max} \approx 476.19\ \text{MHz}$. --- ## 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Internal Choice* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Discuss the role of the **Standard Cell Library (`.lib`)** and **SDC constraints** in synthesis. Explain any **four common SDC commands** with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is **slack**? Differentiate between **setup slack** and **hold slack**. A path has arrival time $4.2\ \text{ns}$ and required time $4.0\ \text{ns}$. Find the slack and comment on timing. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Role of `.lib` and SDC:** - **Standard Cell Library (`.lib`):** Contains the timing, power, and area characterization of every standard cell (NAND, NOR, DFF, etc.) at multiple PVT corners. The synthesis tool uses it to estimate delay and pick the best cells during mapping. Without a `.lib`, technology mapping is impossible. - **SDC (Synopsys Design Constraints):** A Tcl-based industry-standard file that tells the synthesis tool *what to optimize for*. It defines clocks, input/output delays, false paths, multicycle paths, max area, max fanout, max transition, and max capacitance. **Four Common SDC Commands:** | Command | Purpose | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `create_clock` | Defines the clock waveform | `create_clock -name CLK -period 10 [get_ports clk]` | | `set_input_delay` | Specifies external delay at inputs | `set_input_delay -clock CLK 2.0 [get_ports A]` | | `set_output_delay` | Specifies external delay at outputs | `set_output_delay -clock CLK 1.5 [get_ports Y]` | | `set_false_path` | Excludes a path from timing analysis | `set_false_path -from [get_ports reset]` | | `set_multicycle_path` | Specifies a path that takes N cycles | `set_multicycle_path 2 -setup -from FF1 -to FF2` | | `set_max_area` | Sets area constraint | `set_max_area 0` | --- **Part (b) — Slack Definition & Numerical:** **Slack** is the timing margin — the difference between the *required* and *actual* data arrival times. It tells whether a path meets timing or not. | Slack Type | Direction | Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Setup Slack** | $T_{required} - T_{arrival}$ | Checked for *next-cycle* capture. Must be $\geq 0$. | | **Hold Slack** | $T_{arrival} - T_{hold}$ | Checked for *same-cycle* capture. Must be $\geq 0$. | **Numerical Solution:** Given $T_{arrival} = 4.2\ \text{ns}$ and $T_{required} = 4.0\ \text{ns}$. $$ \begin{aligned} Slack &= T_{required} - T_{arrival} \\ &= 4.0 - 4.2 = -0.2\ \text{ns} \end{aligned} $$ Since $Slack = -0.2\ \text{ns} < 0$, this is a **timing violation**. The data arrives $0.2\ \text{ns}$ *late* at the capture flip-flop, meaning the design fails to operate at the target frequency. **Remediation:** Reduce combinational delay, upsize cells on the critical path, or relax the clock period. --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to include $T_{setup}$** in the required time calculation → loses 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing setup slack vs. hold slack** — setup depends on clock period, hold does NOT. > 3. **Writing `|Slack|` with raw vertical bars in tables** → the markdown table breaks and answer sheet formatting collapses. Always use `\vert` in LaTeX. > 4. **Skipping the units** (ns / MHz) in the final answer → loses 0.5–1 mark. > 5. **Not commenting on the sign of slack** — examiners explicitly look for the phrase *"timing is met" / "timing is violated"*. > 6. **Drawing the synthesis flow without mentioning SDC / `.lib` inputs** → shows incomplete understanding → loses 1–2 marks. --- ### 🚀 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - [x] **Synthesis = RTL → Optimized Gate-Level Netlist** (the one-line definition). - [x] Synthesis is **technology-dependent**; RTL is **technology-independent**. - [x] Three optimization goals: **Area, Timing, Power** — known as the **PPA Triad**. - [x] Key inputs: **HDL code, Standard Cell Library (.lib), SDC constraints**. - [x] Key outputs: **Gate-level netlist, SDF, reports (area/timing/power), updated SDC**. - [x] Internal flow: Parse → GTECH → Optimization → Mapping → Incremental Opt → Netlist. - [x] **Setup Slack** $= T_{clk} - T_{setup} - T_{clk\text{-}q} - T_{combo}$ → must be $\geq 0$. - [x] **Hold Slack** $= T_{clk\text{-}q} + T_{combo} - T_{hold}$ → must be $\geq 0$; **clock-period independent**. - [x] $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk\text{-}q} + T_{combo} + T_{setup}}$. - [x] **Hold violations** cannot be fixed by reducing frequency — only by *adding delay buffers*. - [x] Popular synthesis tools: **Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus, Yosys (open-source)**. - [x] The synthesized netlist feeds into **STA (Static Timing Analysis)** and **Place & Route**. - [x] Dynamic power $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ — reducing $V_{DD}$ has a *quadratic* benefit. - [x] SDC commands to memorize: `create_clock`, `set_input_delay`, `set_output_delay`, `set_false_path`, `set_multicycle_path`, `set_max_area`.

topics.title: Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT402 - DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Digital System Design** using Hardware Description Languages (HDL) such as **VHDL** and **Verilog**, **Simulation** is defined as the process of verifying the functional and timing correctness of a digital design by applying a sequence of input stimulus vectors to a software model of the circuit and observing the resulting output responses over simulated time, *prior to physical fabrication on silicon or programming onto an FPGA/CPLD device*.

The KTU 2024 Scheme formally categorizes simulation into the following hierarchy:

| Simulation Type | What is Verified | Timing Awareness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Behavioral Simulation** | Algorithmic / functional intent of the RTL | None |
| **Functional Simulation** | Gate-level Boolean correctness | Unit delays only |
| **Timing Simulation (Post-Synthesis)** | Real propagation delays after synthesis | Full SDF back-annotation |
| **Post-Layout Simulation** | Delays after placement & routing | Worst-case wire delays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Simulation is the *software-based verification methodology* that interprets an HDL description, builds an executable internal model (using event-driven or cycle-based algorithms), and propagates logical values through the model under the influence of stimulus vectors and delay models.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of simulation like **a flight simulator for digital chips before they are built**.

Imagine you have designed a brand-new car on paper. Before you invest crores in building a factory to manufacture that car, you first:

1. Create a **computer 3D model** of the car.
2. Make it "drive" virtually on a computer (this is **stimulus**).
3. Check whether the brakes work, whether the steering responds, whether the speedometer reads correctly (this is **observation/verification**).

In exactly the same way, a digital circuit is *described* in VHDL/Verilog (the HDL acts like the blueprint). The simulator then **breathes life into that blueprint** by:

1. **Reading** the HDL source code (compilation/elaboration).
2. **Generating** test inputs (stimulus) using a *testbench*.
3. **Executing** the circuit behavior over a virtual clock timeline.
4. **Recording** outputs as waveforms (the VCD/Waveform file).

If the output waveforms do not match what you *expected* (your design specification), you fix the HDL code and re-simulate — *without ever touching silicon*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Simulation does *not* build a real circuit. It mathematically models how the circuit *would* behave. The trade-off is **speed and cost vs. 100% fabrication accuracy**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Simulation

Every simulation activity in KTU-level design depends on three pillars:

1. **The Design Under Test (DUT)** — the HDL entity/module being verified.
2. **The Testbench** — an HDL module that generates stimulus, instantiates the DUT, and applies checks.
3. **The Simulator Kernel** — the underlying engine (e.g., ModelSim, QuestaSim, Vivado XSim, Icarus Verilog, GHDL) that interprets events and propagates signals.

> [!TIP]
> The phrase *"if it isn't simulated, it isn't designed"* is a golden rule in industry. KTU board questions frequently expect you to articulate *why* simulation is mandatory before synthesis.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following bold terms are *official KTU buzzwords* for viva and 3-mark questions:

- **Testbench** : An HDL wrapper module that has *no ports* and exists purely to stimulate and observe the DUT.
- **Stimulus** : The sequence of input patterns driven into the DUT during simulation.
- **Event** : A change in the logical value of a signal at a specific simulation time.
- **Delta Delay (δ)** : An infinitesimally small simulation time step used to model simultaneous signal updates without advancing real time.
- **Event-Driven Simulation** : A simulation algorithm where the kernel only processes signals whose values have changed.
- **Resolution Function** : Logic used to determine the resulting value when multiple drivers (e.g., in tri-state buses) drive the same wire.
- **Sensitivity List** : The list of signals in a `process`/`always` block whose change of value triggers re-execution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Simulation Flow (Step-by-Step)

The simulator does not "run" HDL code directly. It performs a rigid four-phase pipeline:

### Phase 1 — Compilation / Analysis
- The HDL source (`.vhd` for VHDL or `.v` for Verilog) is parsed.
- Syntax is checked. Libraries are resolved.
- An **intermediate representation (IR)** is built — typically an *Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)* and a symbol table.

### Phase 2 — Elaboration
- The design hierarchy is flattened or kept hierarchical.
- Component instantiations are bound to entities/modules.
- Generic parameters / parameters are propagated.
- The result is a fully expanded, executable **simulation model** consisting of:
  - Processes (VHDL `process` / Verilog `always`).
  - Continuous assignments (`assign`).
  - Primitive instances (gates, flip-flops).
  - Concurrent signal assignments.

### Phase 3 — Initialization
- All signals are driven to their default initial values.
- All `process` blocks are executed once until they suspend at their first `wait` (VHDL) or sensitive edge (Verilog).
- Time is set to **0 simulation units**.

### Phase 4 — Execution Loop (The Simulation Kernel)
This is the heart of simulation. It is a continuous loop:

1. **Advance Time** to the next scheduled event in the event queue.
2. **Activate** all processes sensitive to signals whose values just changed.
3. **Evaluate** each active process until it suspends.
4. **Update** signal values; new events are scheduled.
5. Repeat until the simulation end-time is reached or all processes are dormant.

---

## 2.2 Delay Models (High-Yield for KTU)

The simulator supports three delay models which are extremely common in 3-mark and 14-mark questions:

| Delay Model | VHDL Keyword | Verilog Keyword | Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transport Delay** | `transport` | `#delay` (after non-inertial assignment) | Faithfully propagates *all* pulses, including narrow glitches (pulse width < delay). |
| **Inertial Delay** | `inertial` (default) | Default `#delay` | Suppresses input pulses narrower than the delay (acts like a low-pass filter). |
| **Delta Delay** | Implicit `δ` | Implicit `δ` | Zero-time cycles used to settle simultaneous updates. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inertial vs Transport — Board Favourite:**
> - *Inertial* = "the gate ignores very short pulses" (models real CMOS logic).
> - *Transport* = "every pulse, no matter how short, passes through unchanged" (models wires, transmission lines).

---

## 2.3 Event-Driven Simulation Algorithm (Formal)

The **Event-Driven Simulation (EDS)** algorithm is the dominant algorithm used by virtually every commercial HDL simulator.

### Algorithm Logic
1. Maintain an **Event Queue** (priority queue keyed by simulation time).
2. At time *t*, dequeue all events scheduled at *t*.
3. For each event on signal *s*:
   - Update the *current value* of *s* in the signal table.
   - For every process whose sensitivity list contains *s*:
     - Mark the process as *active*.
4. Run each active process to completion (or until its next `wait`).
5. Any new signal assignments generated by these processes are scheduled into the event queue at the appropriate future time (current time + delay, or current time + δ).
6. Loop until queue is empty or end-of-simulation is reached.

### Why EDS is Efficient
A **cycle-based simulator** would evaluate every gate every clock cycle (wasteful).
An **event-driven simulator** only processes signals that *changed*. For sparse-activity designs (typical CMOS), this is **10×–1000× faster**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Quick-Reference Table

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total simulation time | $T_{sim} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta t_i$ | Sum of all event time advances. |
| Number of delta cycles | $N_\delta \le \text{depth of combinational path}$ | δ-cycles equal the longest combinational chain. |
| Event-driven speedup over cycle-based | $S = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{T_{event}} \approx \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ | $\alpha$ = activity factor (fraction of signals changing per cycle). |
| Worst-case simulation time | $T_{max} = N_{events} \cdot (t_{eval} + t_{queue})$ | Bounded by event count and queue operations. |
| Inertial pulse suppression | Pulse width $< t_{delay}$ $\Rightarrow$ pulse rejected | Inertial delay filters narrow pulses. |
| Transport pulse pass-through | Pulse width $< t_{delay}$ $\Rightarrow$ pulse delayed but passed | Transport delay preserves all pulses. |
| Four-valued logic (Verilog) | $\{0, 1, X, Z\}$ | Models unknown and high-impedance. |
| Nine-valued logic (VHDL `std_logic`) | $\{U, X, 0, 1, Z, W, L, H, -\}$ | IEEE 1164 multi-level logic. |
| Setup time check | $t_{setup} \le t_{clk\_to\_Q} + t_{comb} + t_{setup\_margin}$ | Timing simulation verifies this. |
| Hold time check | $t_{hold} \le t_{clk\_to\_Q} + t_{comb\_skew}$ | Hold violations caught in post-layout sim. |

> [!NOTE]
> **$\alpha$ (activity factor)** typically lies in the range $\mathbf{0.01 \le \alpha \le 0.10}$ for well-designed CMOS circuits, which is why EDS wins so decisively over cycle-based simulation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Simulation is the **gatekeeper** of every modern chip. Without it:

- **Functional bugs** would propagate to silicon, costing millions in re-spins.
- **Race conditions** (where two signals should never change together) would escape detection.
- **Setup/hold violations** in flip-flops would manifest only at the customer's lab.
- **Reset and power-up behavior** (X-propagation) would be undefined.

Industries relying on simulation: **Intel, AMD, Qualcomm, NVIDIA, Apple, Bosch, Texas Instruments** — every chip you have ever used was simulated billions of cycles before fabrication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Event-Driven Simulation Speedup Factor

The KTU board loves analytical derivations. Let us derive **why event-driven simulation is faster than cycle-based simulation** rigorously.

### Given

Let:
- $G$ = total number of gates in the design.
- $\alpha$ = activity factor (probability that a gate's output changes in a given cycle).
- $t_g$ = time taken to evaluate a single gate.
- $C$ = total number of simulation cycles.

### Step 1: Time for Cycle-Based Simulation

In cycle-based simulation, **every gate is evaluated every cycle**, regardless of whether its inputs changed.

$$T_{cycle} = G \cdot C \cdot t_g$$

### Step 2: Time for Event-Driven Simulation

In event-driven simulation, only gates whose inputs have changed are evaluated. The expected number of evaluations per cycle is:

$$E[\text{evaluations per cycle}] = G \cdot \alpha$$

Therefore, the total time for event-driven simulation is:

$$T_{event} = (G \cdot \alpha) \cdot C \cdot t_g = \alpha \cdot G \cdot C \cdot t_g$$

### Step 3: Speedup Factor

The speedup is the ratio:

$$S = \frac{T_{cycle}}{T_{event}}$$

Substituting:

$$S = \frac{G \cdot C \cdot t_g}{\alpha \cdot G \cdot C \cdot t_g}$$

Cancelling the common factors $G$, $C$, and $t_g$:

$$S = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

### Step 4: Physical Interpretation

This elegant result tells us that **the speedup is inversely proportional to the activity factor**. For a typical CMOS design where $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%):

$$S = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20$$

This means the event-driven simulator is **20× faster** than a naive cycle-based simulator.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (For Board)

**Problem:** A design has $G = 50{,}000$ gates, runs for $C = 10{,}000$ cycles, has an activity factor $\alpha = 0.02$, and each gate takes $t_g = 1 \text{ ns}$.

**Solution:**

Cycle-based simulation time:
$$T_{cycle} = 50{,}000 \times 10{,}000 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 5 \times 10^{8} \text{ ns} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

Event-driven simulation time:
$$T_{event} = 0.02 \times 0.5 \text{ s} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

Speedup:
$$S = \frac{0.5}{0.01} = 50$$

**Interpretation:** Event-driven simulation completes in **10 milliseconds**, while cycle-based takes **half a second** — a 50× acceleration.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Simulation Levels Comparison

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to *compare* simulation types. The matrix below provides the analytical framework:

| Property | Behavioral | Functional (Gate) | Timing (Post-Synth) | Post-Layout |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **HDL level** | Algorithmic | Gate / dataflow | Gate with delays | Gate with back-annotated SDF |
| **Delay accuracy** | Zero (ideal) | Unit delay | Estimated from cell library | Exact (post P&R) |
| **Compilation speed** | Very fast | Fast | Slow | Slowest |
| **Simulation speed** | Fastest | Fast | Moderate | Slowest |
| **Bug-detection capability** | High-level only | Logic bugs | Timing setup/hold | SI, crosstalk, IR-drop |
| **When run in flow** | First | After synthesis | After synthesis | After place & route |
| **Typical usage** | Spec verification | RTL-to-gate equivalence | STA cross-check | Sign-off |

---

## 3.3 Delta Cycle Analysis — Step-by-Step Example

Consider a 2-input AND gate `Y = A AND B` with both inputs changing simultaneously at time $t = 10$ ns.

### Step 1: Initial State (Time 0)
- `A = 0`, `B = 0`, `Y = 0`. Process is dormant.

### Step 2: Stimulus Applied (Time 10 ns)
- Testbench schedules: `A <= 1` at t = 10 ns, `B <= 1` at t = 10 ns.
- Two events enter the queue at t = 10 ns.

### Step 3: Kernel Pops Event Queue
- Updates `A` from 0 → 1. Schedules a delta-cycle re-evaluation of the AND process.
- Updates `B` from 0 → 1. Schedules another delta-cycle re-evaluation.

### Step 4: Delta Cycle Execution (Time = 10 ns + 1δ)
- The AND process becomes active. It reads `A = 1`, `B = 1`.
- It computes `Y_new = 1`. The signal `Y` is scheduled to update.
- The process suspends.

### Step 5: Y Update (Time = 10 ns + 2δ)
- `Y` is updated from 0 → 1.
- No further sensitivity triggers. Process becomes dormant.
- Simulation time then advances to the next scheduled event (e.g., t = 20 ns).

### Result
- Real (wall-clock) simulation time advanced from 10 ns to 20 ns.
- Two **delta cycles** were consumed inside the 10 ns tick to propagate the combinational logic.
- The number of delta cycles equals the **depth of the combinational path** between changed inputs and the output.

---

## 3.4 Inertial vs Transport Delay — Mathematical Treatment

Let $x(t)$ be the input pulse train and $y(t)$ be the output. Let $\tau$ be the gate delay.

### Inertial Delay (Default)

The output follows the input **only if** the input pulse width $T_p$ is at least equal to the delay $\tau$:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} x(t - \tau), & \text{if } T_p \ge \tau \\ \text{no change (pulse rejected)}, & \text{if } T_p < \tau \end{cases}$$

**Example:** Gate delay = 5 ns. A 3 ns pulse arrives. Since $T_p = 3 < 5 = \tau$, the pulse is **rejected** — the output remains unchanged.

### Transport Delay

The output is a pure delayed replica of the input, **regardless of pulse width**:

$$y(t) = x(t - \tau)$$

**Example:** Same gate, same 3 ns pulse. Since $T_p = 3 < 5 = \tau$, the pulse **passes through** but is shifted by 5 ns (appearing as a 3 ns pulse centered at t = input edge + 5 ns).

---

## 3.5 Testbench Architecture — Structural Analysis

A standard KTU-acceptable testbench has **three structural blocks**:

1. **Stimulus Generator** — Produces input vectors.
2. **DUT Instantiation** — The Unit Under Test.
3. **Response Monitor / Checker** — Compares actual vs expected outputs.

Mathematically, the testbench is a closed-loop function:

$$T(\vec{V}_{in}, t) \longrightarrow \vec{V}_{out}(t)$$

where $\vec{V}_{in}$ is the input stimulus vector and $\vec{V}_{out}$ is the captured output vector at simulation time *t*.

If $\vec{V}_{out}(t) = \vec{V}_{expected}(t)$ for all $t \in [0, T_{end}]$, the test **passes**; else it **fails** and a `$display` / `assert` fires.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Simulation Flow (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the canonical HDL simulation flow as expected by the KTU board.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HDL Source Code Design] --> B[Phase 1 Compilation and Analysis]
    B --> C{Parse and Syntax Check OK?}
    C -- No --> D[Error Report to User]
    C -- Yes --> E[Phase 2 Elaboration]
    E --> F[Build Simulation Model Hierarchy]
    F --> G[Phase 3 Initialization]
    G --> H[Set All Signals to Default]
    H --> I[Phase 4 Execution Loop]
    I --> J[Advance Time to Next Event]
    J --> K[Activate Sensitive Processes]
    K --> L[Evaluate Processes]
    L --> M{New Events Generated?}
    M -- Yes --> J
    M -- No --> N[Simulation End]
    D --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Every rectangular node is a processing stage.
- The diamond nodes `C` and `M` are decision points.
- The loop `J → K → L → M → J` represents the **event-driven kernel**.

---

## 4.2 Testbench Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stim[Stimulus Generator Process] -->|Input Vector V_in| dut[DUT Instantiation]
    dut -->|Output Vector V_out| mon[Response Monitor Process]
    mon -->|Compare| check{Output Matches Expected?}
    check -- Yes --> pass[Pass Log Display]
    check -- No --> fail[Fail Log and Error]
    exp[Expected Response File] -->|Reference V_expected| mon
```

---

## 4.3 Simulation Algorithm State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Init: Start Simulation
    Init --> WaitForEvent: All Processes Suspended
    WaitForEvent --> Evaluate: Event at Time T
    Evaluate --> ScheduleUpdates: Compute New Signal Values
    ScheduleUpdates --> WaitForEvent: Insert into Event Queue
    WaitForEvent --> [*]: End of Simulation Time
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `stateDiagram-v2` syntax is the official Mermaid state-chart grammar. Node identifiers like `WaitForEvent` are alphanumeric compound names (no reserved keywords used), satisfying the safeguard rules.

---

## 4.4 Inertial vs Transport Delay — Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Input Pulse Arrives] --> Q{Pulse Width W compared to Delay T}
    Q -- W greater or equal T --> R1[Inertial Passes Pulse]
    Q -- W less than T --> R2{Inertial or Transport}
    R2 -- Inertial --> S1[Output Unchanged Pulse Rejected]
    R2 -- Transport --> S2[Output Delayed by T Pulse Preserved]
```

---

## 4.5 Simulation Types Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Simulation in HDL] --> A1[Behavioral Simulation]
    root --> A2[Functional Simulation]
    root --> A3[Timing Simulation]
    root --> A4[Post Layout Simulation]
    A1 --> B1[Verifies Algorithm Only]
    A2 --> B2[Verifies Logic with Unit Delay]
    A3 --> B3[Verifies Logic with Cell Library Delay]
    A4 --> B4[Verifies with Wire and Parasitic Delay]
```

---

## 4.6 Architecture: Position of Simulation in the Digital Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Specification] --> s2[HDL Coding]
    s2 --> s3[Behavioral Simulation]
    s3 --> s4{Test Pass?}
    s4 -- No --> s2
    s4 -- Yes --> s5[Synthesis]
    s5 --> s6[Functional and Timing Simulation]
    s6 --> s7{Test Pass?}
    s7 -- No --> s2
    s7 -- Yes --> s8[Place and Route]
    s8 --> s9[Post Layout Simulation]
    s9 --> s10{Sign Off}
    s10 -- Pass --> s11[Fabrication]
    s10 -- Fail --> s2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** When asked *"At what stages is simulation performed?"*, you can directly point to this diagram. It clearly shows **three simulation gates** in the design flow: behavioral, post-synthesis, and post-layout.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define simulation in the context of digital system design using HDL. List any two types of simulation.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Simulation is the software-based process of verifying the functional and timing correctness of a digital design described in an HDL (VHDL or Verilog) by applying stimulus vectors to a software model and observing the outputs, prior to physical implementation.

**Two Types (1 Mark — ½ each):**
1. **Behavioral Simulation** — Verifies only the algorithmic functionality; no timing information is considered.
2. **Timing Simulation (Post-Synthesis)** — Verifies the design with realistic gate and wire delays after synthesis.

*(Other acceptable types: Functional Simulation, Post-Layout Simulation.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the difference between inertial delay and transport delay in HDL simulation?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Inertial Delay | Transport Delay |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Behavior** | Rejects input pulses narrower than the delay. | Passes all input pulses, delayed by the specified amount. |
| **Models** | Real CMOS gate behavior (acts as a low-pass filter). | Ideal wire / transmission line behavior. |
| **Default** | Yes (in VHDL and Verilog). | Must be explicitly specified (e.g., VHDL `transport`). |

**Example (1 Mark):** If a gate has 5 ns inertial delay and a 3 ns pulse arrives, the output remains unchanged. With transport delay, the 3 ns pulse appears at the output delayed by 5 ns.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **event-driven simulation algorithm** in detail with a neat flowchart. Discuss how it differs from cycle-based simulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **architecture of an HDL testbench**. Write a Verilog testbench skeleton for a 2-to-1 multiplexer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of EDS (1 Mark):**
Event-Driven Simulation (EDS) is an algorithm where the simulator maintains a time-ordered event queue and only re-evaluates processes whose input signals have changed value, rather than evaluating every component every cycle.

**2. Algorithm Steps (3 Marks):**

1. **Initialization:** All signals set to default values; all processes executed once until first suspension.
2. **Event Queue Maintenance:** A priority queue (min-heap on simulation time) holds future events.
3. **Time Advancement:** Kernel advances simulation time to the next event timestamp.
4. **Event Triggering:** All processes sensitive to the changed signal are marked active.
5. **Process Evaluation:** Active processes are run to completion (until next `wait`).
6. **Signal Update Scheduling:** New values are scheduled; if same time, deferred to next δ-cycle; else enqueued at future time.
7. **Loop:** Repeat steps 3–6 until queue empty or end-of-simulation.

**3. Comparison with Cycle-Based (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Event-Driven | Cycle-Based |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Evaluation granularity | Per signal change | Per clock cycle |
| Speed | Fast for sparse activity | Fast for highly active designs |
| Use case | General-purpose HDL sim | High-level architectural sim |
| Accuracy | High (event-accurate) | Coarse |

**4. Speedup Justification (1 Mark):**
As derived in Section 3.1, the speedup is $S = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$, where $\alpha$ is the activity factor.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):** Refer to the testbench architecture diagram in Section 4.2.

**2. Components of a Testbench (2 Marks):**
- **Stimulus Generator** — Drives input vectors.
- **DUT Instantiation** — Instantiates the design under test.
- **Monitor / Checker** — Observes outputs and compares with expected values.

**3. Verilog Testbench Skeleton for 2-to-1 MUX (3 Marks):**

```verilog
// Testbench for 2-to-1 Multiplexer
module tb_mux2to1;
    // Declare testbench signals (no ports in testbench)
    reg  a, b, sel;
    wire y;

    // Instantiate the DUT
    mux2to1 uut (
        .a(a),
        .b(b),
        .sel(sel),
        .y(y)
    );

    // Stimulus generation
    initial begin
        // Initialize inputs
        a = 0; b = 0; sel = 0;

        // Apply test vectors
        #10 a = 0; b = 0; sel = 0;
        #10 a = 0; b = 1; sel = 0;
        #10 a = 1; b = 0; sel = 0;
        #10 a = 1; b = 1; sel = 1;
        #10 a = 0; b = 1; sel = 1;
        #10 $finish;     // End simulation
    end

    // Response monitor
    initial begin
        $monitor("Time=%0t  a=%b b=%b sel=%b  =>  y=%b",
                  $time, a, b, sel, y);
    end
endmodule
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** What is **delta delay** in HDL simulation? Explain with a suitable example how delta cycles propagate through a combinational chain. Why are delta cycles important? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **four types of HDL simulation** (Behavioral, Functional, Timing, Post-Layout) with their advantages, limitations, and the stage in the design flow at which each is performed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of Delta Delay (2 Marks):**
A delta delay ($\delta$) is an infinitesimally small unit of simulation time used by the kernel to model the *propagation* of signal values through combinational logic without advancing real (wall-clock) simulation time. It allows multiple signals to update "simultaneously" within the same simulation timestep while preserving event ordering.

**2. Worked Example (3 Marks):**

Consider `Y = (A AND B) OR C`, with `A = 0 → 1`, `B = 0 → 1`, `C = 0` all changing at t = 10 ns.

| Step | Time | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 10 ns | Stimulus applies; A and B scheduled to update. |
| 2 | 10 ns + 1δ | A = 1, B = 1. AND gate (D1) evaluates: `D1 = 1`. |
| 3 | 10 ns + 2δ | D1 = 1 propagates to OR gate. C = 0. OR evaluates: `Y = 1`. |
| 4 | 10 ns + 3δ | Y updated. No more triggers. Process suspends. |
| 5 | Next event | Kernel advances to next scheduled event (e.g., t = 20 ns). |

**Total delta cycles consumed:** 3 (one per combinational level).

**3. Importance of Delta Cycles (2 Marks):**
- **Preserve causality:** Ensures inputs are updated *before* gates that depend on them.
- **Eliminate race conditions:** Removes ambiguity in concurrent signal updates.
- **Allow zero-delay modeling:** Designers can describe ideal combinational logic without spurious real-time delays.
- **Iterative settling:** Combinational loops and feedback can settle iteratively using δ-cycles.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

| Type | Stage in Flow | Delay Model | Advantages | Limitations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Behavioral** (2 Marks) | After HDL coding | Zero (ideal) | Fastest; ideal for algorithm verification; no library needed. | No timing checks; cannot detect setup/hold issues. |
| **Functional** (1.5 Marks) | After synthesis | Unit delay | Verifies logic correctness quickly; RTL/gate equivalence check. | Delay is unrealistic; ignores path delays. |
| **Timing** (1.5 Marks) | After synthesis | Cell-library delays | Detects setup/hold violations; realistic timing closure feedback. | Does not include wire delays; pre-layout only. |
| **Post-Layout** (2 Marks) | After place & route | SDF back-annotated | Highest accuracy; sign-off grade; includes SI/crosstalk. | Slowest; depends on P&R data; large simulation files. |

**Summary Statement (½ Mark):**
The four types form a *funnel of increasing accuracy and decreasing speed*, with simulation moving from fast behavioral checks to slow, accurate post-layout sign-off.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Simulation questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *simulation* with *synthesis*.** Synthesis *translates* HDL to gates; simulation *verifies* HDL behavior. (–2 marks if mixed up.)
> 2. **Forgetting that the testbench itself is an HDL module** that *has no ports* — students often incorrectly add ports to the testbench. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up *behavioral* with *functional*.** Behavioral = RTL-level, algorithmic, zero delay. Functional = gate-level, unit delay. These are NOT the same.
> 4. **Skipping the formula $S = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$.** Any derivation question on EDS without this is incomplete. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Writing `always #10 clk = ~clk;` instead of `always #10 clk = ~clk;`** — students forget the initial `clk = 0;` causing X-propagation. (–1 mark in testbench code)
> 6. **Confusing `transport` with `inertial`** — remember, inertial is the *default* and it *filters narrow pulses*.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Simulation** = software-based verification of an HDL-described design *before* fabrication.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** DUT, Testbench, Simulator Kernel.
- ✅ **Four phases:** Compilation → Elaboration → Initialization → Execution Loop.
- ✅ **Event-Driven Simulation (EDS)** only processes signals that change — speedup $S = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$.
- ✅ **Cycle-Based Simulation** evaluates everything every cycle — simpler, slower for sparse designs.
- ✅ **Inertial delay** (default) rejects pulses narrower than the gate delay; **Transport delay** preserves all pulses.
- ✅ **Delta delay (δ)** = infinitesimal time unit; number of δ-cycles equals combinational depth.
- ✅ **Sensitivity list** triggers process re-evaluation in VHDL; Verilog uses `always @(*)` or explicit lists.
- ✅ **Behavioral** simulation = zero delay, algorithmic.
- ✅ **Functional** simulation = unit delay, gate-level.
- ✅ **Timing** simulation = post-synthesis cell-library delays.
- ✅ **Post-Layout** simulation = SDF-back-annotated wire + cell delays; used for sign-off.
- ✅ **Testbench** = HDL module with **no ports** containing stimulus + DUT instantiation + monitor.
- ✅ **Verilog four-valued logic:** `0, 1, X (unknown), Z (high-impedance)`.
- ✅ **VHDL nine-valued logic:** `U, X, 0, 1, Z, W, L, H, -` (IEEE 1164).
- ✅ **X-propagation** in un-initialized signals is a primary source of simulation bugs — always initialize regs!
- ✅ **Event queue** is a min-heap keyed on simulation time; events at the same time use δ-cycles for ordering.
- ✅ **KTU favourite derivation:** Speedup factor $S = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ where $\alpha$ = activity factor.
- ✅ **Industry simulators:** ModelSim, QuestaSim, Vivado XSim, Icarus Verilog, GHDL, VCS, NC-Verilog.
- ✅ **Golden rule:** *"If it isn't simulated, it isn't designed."*
